title: Housing Affordability, Income, and Voting Behavior in the United States, 2007-2022
text:
Hi Christopher,
Thank you for your Research Kick-off Report. Your topic touches on important questions about the relationship between housing affordability, household debt, income, and political behavior, and your personal motivation is clear. You are trying to connect broad structural changes in ownership and financial security to patterns you can actually study with data. This is a worthwhile starting point for a capstone project. I also appreciate that you have already gone out and found several public datasets.
That said, I want to be candid with you: as currently written, the proposal still has a major alignment problem between its motivation, research questions, data sources, and method. The good news is that there is a promising project here. But before this becomes a successful capstone paper, you need to narrow the scope and choose one clear empirical path.


The Main Issue: The Proposal Currently Combines Multiple Projects
Right now, the proposal is trying to do several different things at once:

A political economy question: How do housing affordability, debt, and income relate to voting outcomes?
A household finance question: How are debt burdens, housing costs, and financial distress changing across states over time?
A party-level financial behavior question: Do Republicans and Democrats differ in ownership, debt, and long-run financial choices?

These are all interesting questions, but they involve different units of analysis, different dependent variables, and different empirical strategies. Trying to answer all of them in one paper will likely make the final analysis feel scattered and underdeveloped.
For the capstone paper, you need to choose one primary question and organize the full project around it. Once that question is fixed, you can compare a small number of appropriate model specifications and then decide which approach fits the data and research purpose best.
I see two realistic directions:
Path A: Political economy path
How do state-level housing affordability and household debt burdens relate to voting outcomes in U.S. election years?

Natural unit of analysis: state-election-year
Natural outcome variable: party vote share, turnout, or change in vote share, rather than simply which party won the state
Possible methods to compare: linear panel models for vote share, or other limited dependent variable approaches if you redefine the outcome differently

This path can work, but it requires a tighter redesign of the voting component because election outcomes are observed only in election years, not every year. You would also need to be very precise about whether your objective is explaining variation in vote share or predicting party outcomes.
Path B: Household finance and affordability path
How have housing affordability and debt burdens changed across U.S. states, and which factors are associated with higher financial distress or lower ownership security?

Natural unit of analysis: state-year or state-quarter
Natural outcome variable: delinquency rate, homeownership rate, or another direct measure of financial stress
Possible methods to compare: panel regression models with alternative specifications, depending on the exact dependent variable and data frequency

This path is closer to your stated motivation and is probably the more coherent project with your current data. It also gives you more flexibility to compare several sensible model specifications and identify which one provides the clearest and most credible empirical results.
My honest advice is that Path B is the stronger project unless you are especially committed to the politics question. If you do want to compare multiple models, that is fine, but the comparison should happen within one clearly defined project, not across several different projects at once.



A Critical Data Issue: D1 Does Not Measure General U.S. Voting Behavior
The Harvard Dataverse dataset you call D1 is a dataset of ranked-choice voting elections. That creates a major problem for your current research question.

Ranked-choice voting is used in only a limited set of jurisdictions, so it is not a representative measure of general voting behavior across U.S. states.
The unit of analysis in D1 is not a state-year observation. It is closer to a candidate-round-election observation.
The party_affiliation field refers to the candidate, not the financial behavior of voters in that state.
Many of the jurisdictions in the ranked-choice voting data are cities or localities, not entire states.

So even before you estimate any regression, there is a mismatch between the outcome you want and the data you currently have.
If you want to pursue Path A, you should replace D1 with a dataset that actually measures general election outcomes. Better options include:

MIT Election Data and Science Lab for statewide or county-level election returns
Cooperative Election Study if you want individual-level vote choice or party identification

These alternatives fit the question much better than ranked-choice voting data.



Another Important Data Issue: MSPUS Is Not a State-Level Affordability Measure
Your D3 series, Median Sales Price of Houses Sold for the United States (MSPUS), is a national series. That means it changes over time, but it does not vary across states within a given year.
This matters because your main empirical structure is otherwise state-based. If every state gets the same housing price value in a given year, then MSPUS does not help explain why one state differs from another in that year. Once you include year fixed effects, a national series like MSPUS contributes very little in a state-level regression.
More importantly, home price is not the same thing as housing affordability. Affordability is usually some combination of prices, rents, income, interest rates, and cost burden.
For the capstone paper, replace MSPUS with a more appropriate state-level measure such as:

FHFA state house price indexes
Zillow state-level home value or rent data
A constructed affordability measure such as home price to income, rent to income, or share of households spending more than 30% of income on housing




Next Steps
You are working toward both the presentation and the capstone paper. Focus on one clear research question, a well-defined unit of analysis, and a design that is honest about what your data can and cannot tell you.

Choose one primary question and one primary outcome.
Do not try to study voting behavior, party-level personal finance behavior, and ownership trends all in one paper. Decide whether your paper is mainly about voting outcomes or mainly about financial distress and affordability.
Clarify the unit of analysis and the time structure.
If you choose the voting path, your natural unit is state-election-year, not state-year, because elections do not happen every year in the same form. If you choose the household finance path, a state-year or state-quarter panel is more natural. Right now, your three datasets are operating on different time scales, and that mismatch needs to be fixed before you can merge anything cleanly.
Use a better voting outcome if you stay on the politics path.
Do not code the outcome simply as which party won a state. That throws away too much information. A better outcome would be Republican two-party vote share, Democratic two-party vote share, or change in vote share from the previous election.
If ownership is central to your motivation, add a direct ownership measure.
Your current datasets do not actually measure ownership very well. If the paper is really about how ownership and financial security are changing, then add a direct measure such as homeownership rate from ACS or Census data. Otherwise, the project risks talking about ownership without ever measuring it.
Use direct measures, not rankings.
You mention wanting education rankings by state or county. Rankings are usually a poor regression input because they throw away information and make coefficients harder to interpret. Use direct variables instead, such as the share of adults with a bachelor’s degree or higher.
Write out the regression specification explicitly.
Given your current framing, this project is better suited to inference than to prediction. A useful generic specification would be:
\[
Y_{s,t} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \text{Affordability}_{s,t} + \beta_2 \text{Debt Burden}_{s,t} + \mathbf{X}_{s,t}'\boldsymbol{\gamma} + \alpha_s + \delta_t + \varepsilon_{s,t}
\]
where \(s\) indexes states, \(t\) indexes years or election years, \(Y_{s,t}\) is either a voting outcome (Path A) or a financial distress outcome such as delinquency rate (Path B), \(\alpha_s\) is a state fixed effect, \(\delta_t\) is a year fixed effect, and \(\mathbf{X}_{s,t}\) includes controls such as unemployment, education, age structure, and urbanization.
If you deliberately switch to a prediction project instead, then say that clearly and define the target, holdout strategy, and out-of-sample metric. That would be a different project from the one currently described.
Measure affordability directly and inflation-adjust monetary variables.
If you use debt, income, and home values over a long time span, convert them to real terms. Also consider ratios that are easier to interpret, such as debt-to-income ratio, mortgage debt share, student loan share, or housing cost-to-income burden.
Address causality and confounding carefully.
Expensive states also differ in many other ways: urbanization, age structure, education, migration, industrial composition, and long-run political culture. Those factors can affect both financial conditions and political outcomes. Your paper should discuss these alternative explanations directly and interpret coefficients cautiously.
Treat your ideological prior as a hypothesis, not a premise.
The statement that Republicans tend to carry generational wealth while Democrats tend to build innovational wealth may be an interesting motivation for asking a question, but it cannot be assumed to be true before the analysis begins. Phrase it as something to test, not something already established.
Be careful about ecological fallacy.
State-level data can tell you about relationships across states, but it cannot tell you how individual Republicans or Democrats behave with money. For example, if a state with higher debt also has a higher Democratic vote share, that does not mean Democratic voters in that state personally carry more debt.




Draw on Key Background Readings and Data Sources (Recommended)
For your capstone paper and presentation, you may find it helpful to consult the following sources:


Marble, W. & Nall, C. (2021). “Where Self-Interest Trumps Ideology: Liberal Homeowners and Local Opposition to Housing Development” Journal of Politics, 83(4). (Even liberal U.S. homeowners oppose local housing development due to self-interest (property value protection), revealing how housing status shapes political behavior independently of ideological affiliation.)
Ansell, B., Hjorth, F., Nyrup, J., & Larsen, M. V. (2022). “Sheltering Populists? House Prices and the Support for Populist Parties” Journal of Politics, 84(3). (Provides comparative evidence (Denmark, Sweden, Norway, Finland) showing that negative housing price shocks drive voters toward populist parties, which has theoretical relevance to understanding how housing affordability crises may shift U.S. voting behavior.)
Autor, D., Dorn, D., & Hanson, G. (2013). “The China Syndrome: Local Labor Market Effects of Import Competition in the United States.” American Economic Review, 103(6), 2121-2168. (A useful example of how regional economic shocks can be studied with panel data and careful controls.)
MIT Election Data and Science Lab. Data. (Useful source for statewide and county-level election returns if you pursue the voting path.)
Cooperative Election Study. Data Downloads. (Useful if you want individual-level vote choice or party identification rather than aggregate election returns.)
Federal Housing Finance Agency. House Price Index Datasets. (State-level housing price data that fit a state panel much better than a national series.)
Zillow Research. Housing Data. (Helpful for state, county, and metro measures of home values and rents.)




Questions to Think About as You Refine Your Capstone Paper
You do not need to answer all of these now, but they may help you choose a stronger final direction.
1. Choosing the Core Question

Which question matters more to you: how economic conditions relate to voting, or how affordability and debt shape financial distress and ownership security?
If ownership and financial security are your real motivation, is voting actually the right outcome for this project?

2. Timing and Unit of Analysis

If you pursue the voting path, will you use presidential election years only, all even-year federal elections, or some other election-based panel?
How will you align quarterly debt data with election outcomes or annual housing measures?

3. Causality and Alternative Explanations

If you find that more expensive states vote differently, how will you separate the effect of affordability from education, urbanization, migration, and long-run political culture?
If you find that states with higher debt levels look financially worse, how will you distinguish debt itself from labor market weakness or differences in cost of living?

4. Individual Versus Aggregate Interpretation

What can your state-level data tell you about states as places, and what can it not tell you about individual voters?
Would an individual-level survey design answer your party-differences question more directly than an aggregate state panel?

5. Presentation and Takeaway

What is the one chart or table you want your audience to remember?
If you choose Path B, a clear time series of real debt burdens or delinquency rates by state could be very effective. If you choose Path A, a visual showing changes in affordability against changes in vote share may be more compelling.


Best,
Byeong-Hak Choe

title: Using Predictive Analytics to Outperform Human Judgment in NCAA March Madness Bracket Selection
text:
Hi Aaron,
Thank you for your Research Kick-off Report, and for your thoughtful follow-up email about the train/test split question. This is a strong prediction project. The central idea is not only to predict individual game winners, but to convert those probabilities into a bracket portfolio that can compete in a real pool. That is a meaningful and well-motivated data analytics problem, and your use of historical pool results gives the project a concrete applied setting.
The proposal already shows several strengths. First, you recognize that game prediction and bracket selection are not the same task. Second, you are thinking in terms of probabilities rather than deterministic picks, which is the right starting point for bracket simulation. Third, comparing random forest to logistic regression and gradient boosting is a sensible modeling plan, and your idea of normalizing performance across years is important because bracket pools are strongly affected by how upset-heavy a tournament happens to be.
The main priorities for your capstone paper are to tighten the evaluation design, make the train/test strategy fully time-aware, define what “outperforming human judgment” really means in a measurable way, and explain the bracket diversification rule precisely enough that another analyst could reproduce it.


Addressing Your Email: Train/Test Split, Time-Based Validation, and the Confusion Matrix
This is a very good question, and your instinct is right: you should not evaluate on the same games used to train the model. At the same time, you also do not need to permanently throw away a large share of a relatively small historical dataset.
For this project, the best approach is rolling-origin evaluation (also called expanding-window time-based cross-validation):

Choose a first test year after you have enough seasons to train on, such as 2010.
Train on all seasons before that year.
Predict the held-out tournament year.
Then expand the training window by one year and repeat.

For example:

Train on 2003-2009, test on 2010
Train on 2003-2010, test on 2011
…
Train on 2003-2024, test on 2025

This is the cleanest way to respect time order, maximize training data, and still get honest out-of-sample performance.
So, to your direct question: yes, it is standard to report evaluation metrics from time-based held-out years, then retrain the final model on the full historical dataset once the model class, feature set, and tuning choices are fixed. What would be bad practice is using the full dataset for both model fitting and evaluation, then reporting those in-sample results as if they were true predictive performance.
A few important refinements:

A confusion matrix should be secondary, not primary, because your model outputs probabilities and your downstream task is bracket scoring, not only binary classification at a 0.5 threshold.
Your primary game-level metrics should be log loss or Brier score, plus a calibration curve.
Your primary bracket-level metrics should come from the held-out years in the rolling evaluation, not from brackets generated after fitting on all years.
If you compare random forest, logistic regression, and gradient boosting, make that comparison inside the same rolling evaluation framework. Do not choose the best model using all years and then pretend the held-out results were untouched.

This also addresses your concern about rare upsets. You are not “losing” them by holding out years. Each upset still appears as a real out-of-sample test case in its own tournament year, and then becomes available for later training folds. That is a fairer test than a random split, which would mix past and future tournaments together.
One more practical note: for the earliest seasons, your training window may be too small to support stable tuning. It is reasonable to begin formal evaluation only after a minimum training window, such as 5 to 7 prior tournaments.



Next Steps
You are working toward both the presentation and the capstone paper. Focus on a reproducible prediction pipeline, honest out-of-sample evaluation, and a benchmark definition that matches the real bracket-pool problem.

Define the prediction target and unit of analysis clearly.
The predictive model operates at the game level, not the bracket level. Each observation is a tournament game, and the response variable is whether Team A wins. The bracket is a downstream object built from those game-level probabilities. Make that distinction explicit in the capstone paper.
Write the validation design as a forecasting problem.
For the capstone paper, state the evaluation plan clearly:

use an expanding-window train/test design by tournament year,
generate predictions only from information available before that year’s tournament begins,
aggregate held-out metrics across years.

This matters because team features can accidentally leak information from the tournament itself or from post-selection updates if you are not careful about the cutoff date.
Do not rely on accuracy or the confusion matrix alone.
A model that mostly picks favorites can look good on raw accuracy because favorites win often. But your project depends on probability quality, not only hard classifications. For the capstone paper, separate the evaluation into two layers:

Game-level prediction: log loss, Brier score, calibration, and perhaps accuracy by round.
Bracket-level performance: percentile rank, win rate, expected score relative to the pool median, and probability that at least one submitted bracket lands in the top 10%.

Define “outperforming human judgment” precisely.
Right now the title is broad, but the actual benchmark is more specific. Are you trying to beat:

the average entrant in your dad’s pool?
the pool median?
a consensus bracket based on public pick percentages?
the top quartile of human entries?

I recommend choosing one primary benchmark and two secondary ones. For example, your main result could be whether the best model-generated entry finishes above the pool median more often than a consensus bracket does.
Make the bracket diversification rule reproducible.
“Pick brackets that are varied in points between each other” is too vague at the moment. The capstone paper should define the selection rule precisely. For example:

maximize expected top score across a set of \(k\) submitted brackets,
subject to a minimum pairwise Hamming distance between brackets,
or subject to limits on duplicated Final Four or champion picks.

The key is that another analyst should be able to reproduce your bracket portfolio from your written method.
Protect against leakage in feature construction.
This is one of the biggest technical risks in the project. Be precise about which features are available at bracket lock. Pre-tournament KenPom-style efficiencies are reasonable if they are truly frozen before the first tournament game. But do not use statistics that already reflect tournament play, later injuries, or other information that would not have been available when a real bracket was submitted.
Be careful about the KenPom source and data citation.
In your kickoff report, the KenPom reference points to the homepage, not to the exact historical dataset you plan to use. For the capstone paper:

name the exact dataset or repository,
explain whether it is an official export or a community-compiled historical file,
and note any coverage limitations or licensing constraints.

The same goes for the Kaggle competition data: cite the exact competition version you actually use.
Separate public sources from private evaluation data.
Your dad’s pool history workbook is valuable, but it is not a public source in the same way as Kaggle or KenPom. In the capstone paper, cite it as a private spreadsheet or historical pool archive provided by the organizer, and briefly describe what it contains, how many years it covers, and what cleaning steps were needed.
Add simple baselines.
Your more complex models will be much more convincing if they beat simple, transparent rules. At minimum, include:

a seed-only baseline,
a consensus-picks baseline,
and a logistic regression baseline.

If the random forest or gradient boosting model does not beat these consistently, that is still a useful and interesting result.
Think about generalizability honestly.
Your dad’s pool is a strong applied test case, but it is still one pool with one scoring system and one participant mix. Unless you test more than one pool structure, frame your conclusions as applying to this pool and similar pools, not to “human judgment” in general.




References

Draw on key background and technical references (recommended).
For your capstone paper and presentation, the following are especially useful:

Lopez, M. J., & Matthews, G. J. (2015). “Building an NCAA Men’s Basketball Predictive Model and Quantifying Its Success.” Journal of Quantitative Analysis in Sports, 11(1), 5-12.
Kaggle. (2026). March Machine Learning Mania 2026.
R yardstick. Brier score for classification models
R tidymodels developers. Modeling time series with tidy resampling.
R rsample developers. Time-based Resampling.
R tidymodels developers. An introduction to calibration with tidymodels.
R probably developers. probably package reference.
R probably developers. Uses a logistic regression model to calibrate probabilities.
R probably developers. Uses an Isotonic regression model to calibrate model predictions.
Python scikit-learn developers. Brier score loss.
Python scikit-learn developers. TimeSeriesSplit documentation.
Python scikit-learn developers. Probability calibration user guide.





Questions to Think About as You Refine Your capstone paper
You do not need to answer all of these, but they may help you sharpen the analysis and the presentation narrative.
1. What is the real target of success?

Is the goal to maximize average score, maximize the chance that one bracket cashes, or maximize the chance of finishing first?
Those are different optimization problems. Which one fits the way the pool actually pays out?

2. How many brackets should the model submit?

If the model is allowed multiple entries, what is the marginal value of bracket number 2, 3, or 4?
Does performance improve enough to justify the extra entries, or do the extra brackets mostly duplicate the same ideas?

3. How should you evaluate upsets?

A generic confusion matrix may hide the part of the problem you care about most. Would it be helpful to report performance separately for likely favorites and genuine upset opportunities?
Are there certain seed matchups, such as 8-9, 5-12, or 6-11 games, where the model has a real edge over consensus picks?

4. How stable are your probabilities?

If you slightly change the training window or hyperparameters, do the predicted probabilities change a lot?
If they do, then the bracket portfolio may be less robust than it appears.

5. How much of winning is luck?

Even a strong predictive model may only modestly improve the odds of winning a single pool because late-round outcomes dominate scoring. How will you separate “the model is better” from “this particular year was lucky”?
Could you use repeated tournament simulation to show the distribution of possible pool outcomes under your model?

6. What can you claim at the end of the semester?

A realistic capstone conclusion might not be “the model beats humans every year.”
A stronger and more defensible claim may be something like: “A probability-based bracket portfolio can outperform simple human or consensus baselines on average under this scoring system, but year-to-year results remain highly variable.”


Best,
Byeong-Hak Choe

title: Do Higher Levels of Drug-Related Arrests Reduce Overdose Deaths in New York City Neighborhoods?
text:
Hi Jordan and Jake,
Thank you for your Research Kick-off Report. You group has chosen a timely and important public-policy question: whether drug-related arrests are associated with lower or higher overdose mortality across New York City neighborhoods. The project is well motivated, the stakeholder framing is strong, and the decision to use a neighborhood-by-year panel with fixed effects is a sensible starting point for this topic. Your report also does a good job acknowledging several core limitations up front, especially reverse causality, boundary mismatches, and the fact that arrest counts may reflect policing intensity as much as underlying drug activity.
Overall, this has the makings of a strong capstone project. The biggest priorities going forward are to (1) tighten the empirical strategy, (2) lock down a feasible and internally consistent geography, (3) make the outcome model fit the structure of the data, and (4) expand the reference base beyond CDC WONDER so the data plan is more clearly tied to NYC-specific sources.


Next Steps
You are working toward both the presentation and the capstone paper. Focus on clear causal reasoning, a defensible data-construction plan, and transparent interpretation of what your model can and cannot establish.

Address reverse causality more explicitly and place it at the center of your analysis.
Your current limitations section correctly notes reverse causality, but this issue deserves to be central in the Capstone Paper. Neighborhoods experiencing more overdoses may receive more police attention, so a simple positive correlation between arrests and overdose deaths does not imply arrests worsen outcomes. But it may just reflect targeted enforcement. In the Capstone Paper:

State clearly that the main threat to causal interpretation is that enforcement responds to the same drug-market conditions that drive overdoses.
Explain what lagged arrest rates do and do not solve. A one-year lag helps reduce the concern that this year’s arrests are directly caused by this year’s overdose deaths, but it does not eliminate the possibility that both are being driven by the same underlying neighborhood conditions.
Discuss whether there is a policy shift, administrative change, or other real-world event that created variation in arrests that was unrelated to overdose trends. Such variation would give you a stronger basis for a causal claim.

Choose one geography early, and make sure the two datasets are measuring the same place concept.
Right now the paper leaves open ZIP codes versus community districts. That choice is not a minor detail; it may determine whether the project is feasible.

Overdose deaths are often reported by residence, while arrest data are often tied to the location of the arrest. If those are not aligned, the interpretation becomes much weaker.
NYC overdose reporting is often organized at the United Hospital Fund (UHF) neighborhood level, which may be a more practical compromise than raw ZIP codes or community districts.
In the Capstone Paper, explain exactly how you will map arrests and overdose deaths to the same geographic unit, and what you will do if some boundaries do not line up cleanly.

Revisit the outcome model before you commit to log(rate + constant) as the main specification.
The log transformation is a reasonable first idea, but this setting has a few complications:

Small-area overdose counts can be sparse, and some neighborhood-year cells may be zero, suppressed, or unstable.
Adding a constant before taking logs can affect interpretation, especially when values are small.
A useful robustness check would be a fixed-effects Poisson model with a population offset, since the underlying data are counts and Poisson-type models handle zeros more naturally.

If you keep the log-rate model as the main specification, explain the constant you add, test sensitivity to that choice, and compare results to at least one count-based alternative.
Use an inference strategy that matches panel data.
Your attention to robust standard errors is good, but I would rethink Newey-West as the primary correction.

In a neighborhood-by-year panel, clustering standard errors by neighborhood is the more natural default.
If you worry about common shocks across neighborhoods in a given year, you might also discuss two-way clustering by neighborhood and year.
Newey-West can still appear as a robustness check, but it should not be your first or only answer to serial correlation in panel data.

Narrow the time question to a feasible window.
The pre/post-COVID comparison is strong, but the reference to the “80’s” makes the scope feel too broad for the available data and for a capstone timeline.

Unless you find a surprisingly rich historical neighborhood series, the 1980s are probably outside the feasible scope of this project.
A modern window such as the 2010s through the post-COVID years is more realistic and more policy relevant.
In addition to COVID, think about the transition to a fentanyl-dominated overdose environment. The relationship between arrests and overdose deaths may differ sharply between earlier heroin-dominant years and later fentanyl-heavy years, when even small disruptions in supply can lead to users encountering more dangerous substitutes.

Refine the socioeconomic heterogeneity question.
The idea is good, but the current framing, such as “high gini like Manhattan, compared to Staten Island,” needs tightening.

Manhattan and Staten Island are boroughs, not neighborhood categories.
Borough-level inequality is not the same thing as neighborhood-level inequality.
A cleaner approach would be to define heterogeneity using neighborhood poverty rate, median household income, or a small number of income/poverty quartiles drawn from ACS data.

Be careful with ACS controls in a year-by-year fixed-effects panel.
This is a subtle but important data issue.

Small-area ACS estimates are often based on 5-year rolling samples, so adjacent years overlap heavily.
That means year-to-year changes in poverty, income, or unemployment may look more precise than they really are.
Keep the control set parsimonious, and explain how ACS timing and overlap affect interpretation.

Make the possession vs. distribution question operational.
This is one of the most interesting parts of the paper, but it will only work if the arrest data truly allow that split.

Confirm that the NYPD arrest data contain enough charge detail to separate possession from sale/distribution in a defensible way.
If they do, estimate them as separate regressors rather than pooling all drug arrests together.
If they do not, say so early and simplify the project rather than keeping a sub-question you cannot answer cleanly.

Strengthen and localize the data sources.
The current reference list is too thin for the ambitions of the project.

CDC WONDER is useful for national or county-level mortality work, but it is not the ideal source for a neighborhood-level NYC panel.
Your Capstone Paper should reference the NYC Department of Health and Mental Hygiene overdose surveillance pages and the relevant NYC Open Data arrest datasets directly.
Expand the literature review so the project is grounded not only in data availability, but also in current thinking about overdose surveillance, synthetic opioids, and harm reduction.

Include simple descriptive visuals in both the presentation and capstone paper.
A few basic visuals would make the project much easier to understand:

A map of the chosen neighborhood unit showing overdose death rates.
A citywide time-series of overdose deaths and drug arrests.
A scatter plot of neighborhood arrest intensity against overdose mortality.
A small table showing how many neighborhood-year observations remain after merging, suppression handling, and missing-data decisions.





Questions to Think About as You Refine Your Capstone Paper
You do not need to answer all of these now, but they may help you sharpen the design.
1. Causality and Interpretation

Suppose you estimate a positive coefficient on arrests. What is the most credible interpretation: a harmful effect of enforcement, or policing simply responding to higher-risk neighborhoods?
What kind of real-world event or policy change would make you more comfortable saying the association you estimate is close to causal, rather than just descriptive?

2. Geography and Measurement

Are your arrest data measuring where the arrest happened, where the person lives, or something else?
Are your overdose data measuring decedent residence, location of death, or both?
If the two datasets are on different geographic concepts, what is your plan for reconciling them?

3. Functional Form and Robustness

How many neighborhood-year cells have zero, missing, or suppressed overdose values?
Do your headline results change if you compare:

log(overdose rate + constant),
a Poisson count model with a population offset, and
a simpler level-level or standardized-rate specification?


4. Controls and Panel Structure

Which socioeconomic controls actually vary enough within neighborhoods over time to matter in a fixed-effects model?
Are you at risk of “over-controlling” with a long list of highly correlated variables that mostly move together?

5. Policy Narrative

If arrests are not associated with lower overdose mortality, what alternative investments would the evidence point toward?
If arrests appear protective in some places but not others, what neighborhood characteristics seem to condition that relationship?
How will you explain your results to an audience split between public-health and law-enforcement stakeholders?




Background Sources Worth Using in the Capstone Paper
These are not the only sources you should use, but they are better aligned with your project than the current reference list and will help you ground the paper in NYC-specific data and policy context.

New York City Department of Health and Mental Hygiene. NYC Drug and Alcohol Data
New York City Department of Health and Mental Hygiene. Epi Data Briefs and Data Tables
NYC Open Data. NYPD Arrests Data (Historic) - Dangerous Drugs
U.S. Census Bureau. American Community Survey 5-Year Data

title: The Impact of Employee Benefits on Labor Turnover in the United States
text:
Hi Hayden,
Thank you for your Research Kick-off Report. Your topic is practically relevant and easy to motivate. Employee turnover is costly, and understanding whether benefits are associated with lower turnover is a question that matters to managers, compensation analysts, and policy discussions. I also like that you are trying to build the project around public BLS data rather than relying mainly on proprietary surveys.
The proposal shows several strengths. First, you have a clear business problem. Second, you identified useful national data sources. Third, you correctly recognized that this is better suited to regression-style analysis than clustering. The idea of decoding BLS series IDs and building a merged analytic file is also ambitious in a good way.
The main priorities for your Capstone Paper are to sharpen the outcome variable, rethink the unit of analysis around what the BLS datasets actually provide, choose whether the project is primarily inferential or predictive, and make the data-merging plan more concrete.


Next Steps
You are working toward the presentation, the Capstone Paper, and ultimately the capstone paper. Focus on feasible data construction, a clear modeling goal, and careful interpretation.

Define the dependent variable more precisely. Right now the report uses “turnover” and “churn” somewhat interchangeably. In the BLS JOLTS (Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey) data, you will need to decide whether your outcome is:

the quits rate
the total separations rate
or some other JOLTS measure

For a question about employee retention, quits rate or total separations rate makes more sense than job openings or hires. Be explicit about which one you will study and why.
Rethink the unit of analysis around the data that are actually available. Your proposal says the unit of analysis will be “the employee benefits themselves.” That is not the unit of observation in a regression. Benefits are variables. The unit of observation is the row of the dataset.
A more important issue is feasibility. In the public BLS series formats, the Employee Benefits (NB) and Employer Costs for Employee Compensation (ECEC) (CM) series IDs include both industry and occupation, but JOLTS series IDs are organized around industry, geography, establishment size, and data element, not occupation. That means a public JOLTS-based outcome is naturally an industry-by-time outcome, not an industry-by-occupation-by-time outcome.
Because of this, your most feasible unit of analysis is probably an industry-year or industry-month panel. If occupation is central to your question, then you should either:

find a different outcome source that supports occupation-level turnover, or
narrow the project so that the main analysis is industry-level, while occupation appears only in descriptive background tables.

Choose whether the project is inferential or predictive. The capstone should make either an inferential contribution or a predictive contribution, and your Capstone Paper should state which one you are doing.
For this topic, an inferential study seems more natural. Your question is essentially: which benefits are associated with lower turnover, after accounting for other factors? If that is the goal, then your main outputs are coefficient estimates, uncertainty measures, and careful interpretation.
A predictive version is also possible, but it would need a different framing: for example, using benefit and workforce characteristics to predict future turnover rates across industries. If you choose that path, then you would want a training/test split and prediction metrics such as RMSE. In that case, you should avoid strong causal language.
Think carefully about causality, but keep your claims modest. A negative relationship between benefits and turnover does not automatically mean that benefits cause lower turnover. High-turnover industries may offer fewer benefits because of their workforce structure, cost pressures, or business model. Wages, unionization, establishment size, and worker composition may also affect both benefits and turnover.
For the Capstone Paper, you should:

explain this challenge in plain language
state that your regression estimates will show associations unless your design gives stronger causal leverage
include strong control variables where possible
consider using lagged benefit variables, such as benefits in year \(t - 1\) to explain turnover in year \(t\)

Do not restrict the study to a single March snapshot unless you must. Your report sometimes sounds like the analysis will be “as of March 2025.” That is workable for a descriptive comparison, but it is weaker for inference or prediction. If the data coverage allows it, a multi-year panel will be much stronger because it gives you more observations and lets you separate persistent industry differences from broader time shocks.
Write the model down explicitly. Right now the method section says “general Cause-and-Effect regression,” but the actual specification is not written out. Your Capstone Paper should present an equation.
If you use an industry-year panel, a sensible inferential specification would look like:
\[
\begin{align}
\text{TurnoverRate}_{i,t} = \beta_0 &+ \beta_1 \text{HealthAccess}_{i,t} + \beta_2 \text{RetirementAccess}_{i,t} + \beta_3 \text{PaidLeaveCost}_{i,t} \\
&+ \mathbf{X}_{i,t}'\boldsymbol{\gamma} + \alpha_i + \delta_t + \varepsilon_{i,t}
\end{align}
\]
where \(i\) indexes industries, \(t\) indexes time, \(\alpha_i\) captures persistent differences across industries, \(\delta_t\) captures common time shocks, and \(\mathbf{X}_{i,t}\) includes controls such as wage levels, establishment size mix, or other workforce characteristics.
If you choose a predictive framing instead, say so clearly and explain the target variable, feature set, and evaluation plan.
Be explicit about the time alignment problem. Your three main data sources do not operate on the same schedule:

JOLTS is monthly
NB is annual
CM is quarterly

That means you need a clear plan for aligning time. One reasonable option is to aggregate JOLTS to the year level and match it to annual benefit measures. Another is to use March or annual averages consistently if that matches the benefit reference period better. Do not leave this as an unstated merge issue. It is one of the central design choices in the project.
Specify which benefits you will include. “Benefits” is too broad as written. For the Capstone Paper, list the exact benefit measures you plan to study, such as:

medical care access
retirement plan access
paid vacation access
paid sick leave access
employer benefit cost shares from ECEC

Then decide whether you will:

include these as separate regressors, or
build a smaller number of indices, such as a health or insurance index and a leave index

Also note that some benefit variables are likely to move together. You should plan to check pairwise correlations and whether the regression results become unstable when too many similar variables are included at once.
Make the merge plan concrete and test it early. One of the strongest technical parts of your proposal is the idea of decoding BLS series IDs. That can work, but only if you document the exact keys.
For the Capstone Paper, show:

one example NB series ID and how you will parse it
one example CM series ID and how you will parse it
one example JOLTS series ID and how you will parse it
the common fields that will actually be used for the merge

My advice is to build a small prototype dataset first with just a few industries and a few years. That will tell you quickly whether the full design is feasible.
Use non-BLS sources carefully. The QuickBooks and Nuveen materials are fine for motivation, but they should not drive your main empirical analysis. The same caution applies to the GIGAcalculator spreadsheet unless you can clearly document where the data come from and how the measures were constructed.
For the capstone paper, the strongest version of this project uses BLS data as the core analytic source and treats outside surveys as context only.
Strengthen the writing of the research question. Right now the question asks, “Which employee benefits result in the lowest average turnover?” That wording sounds stronger than your design can likely support. A more careful version would be:

Which employee benefits are most strongly associated with lower labor turnover across U.S. industries?

If you later develop a stronger research design, you can revisit the wording. For now, this phrasing is more defensible.




Questions to Think About as You Refine Your Capstone Paper
You do not need to answer all of these now, but they may help you sharpen both the analysis and the story you tell in your presentation and the capstone paper.
1. What exactly is your outcome measure?

Is the best retention outcome the quits rate, the total separations rate, or something else?
Would your interpretation change depending on which turnover measure you use?

2. Can you really study occupations with your chosen outcome data?

If JOLTS is the main source for turnover, how will you support the occupation part of the question?
Would it be better to narrow the project to industries first and do that well?

3. Access versus cost

Do you expect access to benefits or employer spending on benefits to matter more?
Could a low-cost but widely available benefit matter more for retention than a more expensive but rarer one?

4. Timing

Do benefits in the same year explain turnover in that year, or should benefits in one period predict turnover in the next period?
Would lagging the benefits make your interpretation more convincing?

5. Managerial usefulness

Suppose you find that paid sick leave and retirement access are the strongest predictors of lower turnover. How would you turn that into a recommendation for a manager with a limited budget?
Can you rank benefits by approximate “turnover reduction per dollar” rather than only by statistical significance?




Recommended Background Readings

Lazear, E. P., & Shaw, K. L. (2007). “Personnel Economics: The Economist’s View of Human Resources.” Journal of Economic Perspectives, 21(4), 91-114.
Bureau of Labor Statistics. Handbook of Methods: Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey.
Bureau of Labor Statistics. Handbook of Methods: Employee Benefits.
Bureau of Labor Statistics. Handbook of Methods: Employer Costs for Employee Compensation.
Bureau of Labor Statistics. Employee Benefits in the United States, March 2025.
Bureau of Labor Statistics. Series ID Formats.
Ouimet, P., & Tate, G. (2023). “Firms with Benefits? Nonwage Compensation and Implications for Firms and Labor Markets.” NBER Working Paper 31463.
Frazis, H., & Loewenstein, M. (2013). “How Responsive are Quits to Benefits?” Journal of Human Resources, 48(4), 969-997.
Bailey, J., Colman, G. & Dave, D. (2022). “The Evolution of Job Lock in the U.S.: Evidence from the Affordable Care Act” Journal of Risk and Financial Management, 15(7), 296.


Best,
Byeong-Hak Choe

title: Wildfires, Insurer Withdrawal, and the Expansion of FAIR Plans in the Western United States
text:
Hi Ann,
Thank you for your Research Kick-off Report. You have chosen a timely and policy-relevant topic. The retreat of private insurers from wildfire-exposed areas and the growth of FAIR Plan coverage is one of the clearest ways climate risk is reshaping household finance, insurance regulation, and local housing markets in the western United States. Your research question is clear, your stakeholder motivation is strong, and your county-year panel structure is a sensible starting point for this kind of project. I also appreciate that you already recognize practical challenges such as FAIR Plan data locked in PDFs and the difficulty of measuring insurer withdrawal directly.
Overall, this has the makings of a strong capstone project. The biggest priorities for your Capstone Paper are to tighten the data construction plan, decide whether the project is mainly about inference or prediction, and make sure the panel model matches the type of wildfire variable you want to use. Right now, the strongest path is probably a California-first study that leans on official FAIR Plan and California Department of Insurance data, with other western states treated as a secondary comparison only if comparable public data are truly available.


Next Steps
You are working toward both the presentation and the capstone paper. Focus on careful data construction, a well-specified panel model, and interpretation that speaks to insurance regulators and state government.

Turn insurer withdrawal into a measurable outcome, not just a limitation.
Your report says that private insurer withdrawal data is not very public, which is partly true, but the project may be more feasible than you think. The California Department of Insurance now publishes annual homeowners policy counts by county and ZIP code, including new, renewed, and non-renewed policies. That gives you a direct way to measure two different parts of market retreat:

Non-renewals, which capture existing policyholders being dropped from the standard market.
Fewer new policies, which capture insurers becoming less willing to write new business.

For the Capstone Paper, decide which of these is central to your story. I see three promising empirical framings:

wildfire risk and fire activity predict FAIR Plan growth
wildfire risk and fire activity predict private-market non-renewals
wildfire risk and fire activity predict non-renewals, which then help explain FAIR Plan growth

The third option is especially compelling because it matches the policy story most closely.
Use the most direct FAIR Plan data source available.
Your kick-off report emphasizes annual reports in PDF form. That may still be useful, but the California FAIR Plan now publishes a Key Statistics & Data page with county and ZIP-code tables for policies in force and exposure, updated quarterly. This may reduce the data-cleaning burden a lot. For the Capstone Paper:

State whether you will use quarterly FAIR Plan data aggregated to the year level or one annual snapshot per year.
Be explicit about whether your main outcome is policy count, total insured value, or both.
Treat total insured value carefully. It rises not only when more properties enter the FAIR Plan, but also when insured values rise because of housing prices, rebuilding costs, and inflation.

I would lean toward policy count as the cleaner main outcome, and use total insured value as a secondary outcome.
Choose a primary lane: inference or prediction.
Right now your evaluation plan mixes both. That is not wrong, but the paper will be much stronger if you say which one comes first.

If your main goal is inference, then the core result is a panel regression that estimates how wildfire exposure, recent fires, and insurer retreat are associated with FAIR Plan growth after controlling for county and year differences. In that case, the key outputs are coefficients, confidence intervals, and interpretation.
If your main goal is prediction, then the main question is whether wildfire and insurance variables improve out-of-sample forecasts of FAIR Plan enrollment. In that case, the key outputs are RMSE and validation over time.

Given your motivation, I think inference should be primary and prediction should be a short extension. That framing fits regulators better. Also, a county-year dataset is not huge, so more complex machine learning methods may add less than you expect unless you move to a finer geography such as ZIP-year.
Match the panel model to the type of wildfire measure you use.
This is the most important modeling issue in your report. A county fixed-effects model learns from within-county change over time. That means a wildfire variable that is mostly fixed for each county, such as a broad structural risk category, may not do much work once county fixed effects are included. So for the Capstone Paper, be very clear about which wildfire measure is actually changing over time.
A strong starting model would be something like:
\[
\log(1 + \text{FAIRPolicies}_{it}) = \beta_1 \text{FireActivity}_{i,t-1} + \beta_2 \text{NonRenewals}_{it} + \mathbf{X}_{it}'\gamma + \alpha_i + \delta_t + \varepsilon_{it}
\]
where:

\(\alpha_i\) is a county fixed effect
\(\delta_t\) is a year fixed effect
\(\mathbf{X}_{it}\) is a set of county-year controls

This setup fits your project well because recent fire activity and non-renewals both vary over time. If you also want to use a more stable wildfire risk measure, think about using it as:

a baseline county characteristic in a simpler cross-county analysis, or
an interaction term with time, such as baseline risk multiplied by recent fire activity or by a post-2017 indicator.

Also consider lagged fire variables. Insurance renewals and FAIR Plan take-up may respond with a delay after a major fire season.
Use the right denominator and reconsider growth rates.
Population is a reasonable background control, but property insurance is much more naturally tied to housing units and owner-occupied units than to total population. For the Capstone Paper, think about outcomes such as:

FAIR Plan policies per 1,000 housing units
FAIR Plan policies per 1,000 owner-occupied units
log policy count
log total insured value

I would be cautious about using growth rates as the main outcome. Growth rates can become noisy in counties where FAIR Plan enrollment starts very small. Log counts are usually easier to interpret and more stable.
Expand the control variables beyond population.
You already know this needs work, and you are right. A stronger county-year control set would include:

median home value
owner-occupied housing share
median household income
housing stock age
number of housing units or new housing permits

These matter because FAIR Plan growth can reflect both wildfire risk and the economic scale of the housing market. Expensive counties can have rising FAIR exposure even if policy counts are not rising as quickly.
Use wildfire incident data that matches your unit of analysis.
The NOAA Climate at a Glance page is useful for background climate context, but it might not be the cleanest source for county-level wildfire activity. For a county-year panel, you would likely be better off with:

CAL FIRE Fire Hazard Severity Zones for structural wildfire exposure (data available under State Responsibility Area data, Click here)
CAL FIRE Fire Perimeters for annual realized fire activity
possibly MTBS if you want burn severity measures

One practical idea is to aggregate annual acres burned, number of large fires, or fire perimeter area to the county-year level. That would match your panel structure well.
Be realistic about the cross-state comparison.
Comparing California with Oregon or Colorado is interesting, but I would treat that as secondary until you confirm the data.

Oregon does have an official FAIR Plan, but California appears to have much richer public statistical releases.
Colorado’s FAIR Plan is very new, which means the time series is likely too short for a strong panel comparison.

Because of that, I would make California the clear primary contribution. If you later confirm comparable data elsewhere, you can add a smaller comparison section.
Use stronger source choices in the capstone paper bibliography.
Your current references point in the right direction, but the Capstone Paper should lean much more heavily on:

official data sources
peer-reviewed research
regulator publications

Trade-press articles can still help motivate the topic, but they should not carry the empirical foundation of the project.
Include a few simple descriptive visuals.
A small set of visuals would make the project much easier to understand:

a map of California counties showing FAIR Plan policy counts or growth
a time-series plot of statewide non-renewals and statewide FAIR Plan policies
a scatter plot of county wildfire risk share versus FAIR Plan growth, with notable counties labeled





References

Draw on key background readings and data sources.
For your Capstone Paper and presentation, I recommend starting with the following sources:

California FAIR Plan Association. Key Statistics & Data
California Department of Insurance. Data and Analysis on Wildfires and Insurance
California Department of Forestry and Fire Protection. Fire Hazard Severity Zones
California Department of Forestry and Fire Protection. Fire Perimeters
U.S. Census Bureau. American Community Survey
Auer, M. R. (2024). “Wildfire risk and insurance: research directions for policy scientists” Policy Sciences, 57(2), 459-484.
Bayham et. al. (2022) “The Economics of Wildfire in the United States” Annual Review of Resource Economics, 14, 379-401
Boomhower, J., Fowlie, M., & Plantinga, A. J. (2023). “Wildfire Insurance, Information, and Self-Protection” AEA Papers and Proceedings, 113, 310-315.
Fowlie et. al. (2025). “How is climate change impacting home insurance markets?”. Brookings Institution

Keys, B. (2025) “Housing, Climate Risk, and Insurance”. NBER Working Paper
Keys, B. & Mulder, P. (2025). “Property Insurance and Disaster Risk: New Evidence from Mortgage Escrow Data”. NBER Working Paper




Questions to Think About as You Refine Your Capstone Paper
You do not need to answer all of these, but they may help you sharpen your analysis and policy story.
1. Data Construction

Can you build a direct measure of insurer retreat using non-renewals and new policy counts from CDI, instead of treating insurer withdrawal as almost fully unobserved?
Will you use county-year as the final unit of analysis, or does the availability of FAIR Plan and insurance data by ZIP code make a ZIP-year panel worth considering?
How will you line up quarterly FAIR Plan data with annual CDI and ACS data? Will you average quarters, use end-of-year values, or define one annual snapshot?

2. Model Choice and Timing

If you keep county fixed effects, which wildfire variable actually changes enough over time to identify the relationship you care about?
Are insurers reacting mostly to recent fires, to baseline structural risk, or to both?
Should recent fire activity enter with a one-year lag, given that insurance renewal decisions often happen after a fire season rather than during it?

3. Outcome Definition

Which outcome best matches your headline question: FAIR Plan policy count, FAIR Plan total insured value, non-renewals, or some sequence across all three?
If total insured value rises, how much of that is more FAIR enrollment versus simply higher insured values in expensive counties?

4. Inference versus Prediction

If your main goal is inference, what is the single coefficient or relationship that a regulator should care most about?
If your main goal is prediction, is the county-year sample large enough to justify machine learning, or would a simpler panel model be more credible and easier to explain?
If you keep a prediction component, would a rolling out-of-time validation be more convincing than one train/test split?

5. Policy Interpretation

If wildfire risk remains strongly associated with FAIR Plan growth even after recent fire activity is controlled for, would that suggest that insurers are reacting more to expected future risk than to recent realized losses?
If private-market non-renewals rise first and FAIR Plan enrollment rises after, how should regulators respond earlier in that sequence?
Do you expect the relationship to differ between Northern California and Southern California, or between coastal and inland counties?


Best,
Byeong-Hak Choe

title: Classwork 3
text:
library(tidyverse)
library(plm)
library(lmtest)
library(sandwich)
library(stargazer)

co2 <- read_csv("https://bcdanl.github.io/data/owid-co2-data.csv")
codebook <- read_csv("https://bcdanl.github.io/data/owid-co2-codebook.csv")

title: Classwork 3
text:
Build a clean country-year panel

df <- co2 |>
  # Keep only country observations (drop regions / aggregates)
  # OWID country entries typically have a 3-letter ISO code
  filter(!is.na(iso_code), nchar(iso_code) == 3) |>
  # Keep useful sample years (adjust as you want)
  filter(year >= 1990) |>
  # Select a simple EKC-style set of variables
  mutate(
    gdp_per_capita = gdp / population, 
    .after = gdp
  ) |> 
  select(
    iso_code, country, year,
    co2_per_capita,
    gdp_per_capita,
    population,
    energy_per_capita,         # optional control
    primary_energy_consumption # optional control (can be NA a lot)
  ) |>
  # Drop missing core variables
  filter(!is.na(co2_per_capita), !is.na(gdp_per_capita)) |>
  # Create logs and squared log GDP per capita
  mutate(
    log_co2pc = log(co2_per_capita),
    log_gdppc = log(gdp_per_capita),
    log_gdppc2 = log_gdppc^2,
    log_pop = log(population),
    log_energy_pc = ifelse(!is.na(energy_per_capita) & energy_per_capita > 0,
                          log(energy_per_capita), 
                          NA)
  ) |>
  # Keep rows with finite log outcomes/predictors
  filter(is.finite(log_co2pc), 
         is.finite(log_gdppc), 
         is.finite(log_gdppc2), 
         is.finite(log_pop))

# Convert to pdata.frame (panel format for plm)
df_panel <- pdata.frame(df, index = c("iso_code", "year"))

title: Classwork 3
text:
Specify models

# Baseline (no optional controls)
f_base <- log_co2pc ~ log_gdppc + log_gdppc2

# Extended model (adds energy per capita; will drop rows with missing log_energy_pc)
f_ext  <- log_co2pc ~ log_gdppc + log_gdppc2 + log_energy_pc

# --- Models (BASE) ---
m1_pool <- plm(f_base, data = df_panel, model = "pooling")                 # pooled OLS
m2_fe   <- plm(f_base, data = df_panel, model = "within", effect = "individual")  # country FE
m3_twfe <- plm(f_base, data = df_panel, model = "within", effect = "twoways")     # country FE + year FE
m4_re   <- plm(f_base, data = df_panel, model = "random", effect = "individual") # random effects

# --- Models (EXTENDED) ---
# (Optional) include energy per capita as a control
m1_pool_ext <- plm(f_ext, data = df_panel, model = "pooling")
m2_fe_ext   <- plm(f_ext, data = df_panel, model = "within", effect = "individual")
m3_twfe_ext <- plm(f_ext, data = df_panel, model = "within", effect = "twoways")
m4_re_ext   <- plm(f_ext, data = df_panel, model = "random", effect = "individual")

title: Classwork 3
text:
Robust / clustered standard errors

# Function to compute clustered SE (country-clustered)
se_cluster_country_safe <- function(model) {
  V <- vcovHC(model, type = "HC1", cluster = "group")
  se <- sqrt(diag(V))
  se[is.na(se)] <- 0              # ✅ replace NA SEs so stargazer won't crash
  return(se)
}

# Clustered SEs for BASE models
se_m1 <- se_cluster_country_safe(m1_pool)
se_m2 <- se_cluster_country_safe(m2_fe)
se_m3 <- se_cluster_country_safe(m3_twfe)
se_m4 <- se_cluster_country_safe(m4_re)

# Clustered SEs for EXTENDED models
se_m1e <- se_cluster_country_safe(m1_pool_ext)
se_m2e <- se_cluster_country_safe(m2_fe_ext)
se_m3e <- se_cluster_country_safe(m3_twfe_ext)
se_m4e <- se_cluster_country_safe(m4_re_ext)

title: Classwork 3
text:
Panel diagnostics

# FE vs pooled OLS (is panel structure important?)
pFtest(m2_fe, m1_pool)


    F test for individual effects

data:  f_base
F = 193.79, df1 = 163, df2 = 5243, p-value < 2.2e-16
alternative hypothesis: significant effects

# RE vs pooled OLS (Breusch-Pagan LM test)
plmtest(m1_pool, type = "bp")


    Lagrange Multiplier Test - (Breusch-Pagan)

data:  f_base
chisq = 42925, df = 1, p-value < 2.2e-16
alternative hypothesis: significant effects

# FE vs RE (Hausman test)
phtest(m2_fe, m4_re)


    Hausman Test

data:  f_base
chisq = 9819.7, df = 2, p-value < 2.2e-16
alternative hypothesis: one model is inconsistent

title: Classwork 3
text:
Empirical EKC

# EKC shape + 95% band (country-clustered), plotted over x in [0, 20], y in [0, 13]

# 0) Cluster-robust vcov
V <- vcovHC(m3_twfe_ext, type = "HC1", cluster = "group")

# 1) Coefs (GDP terms only)
b <- coef(m3_twfe_ext)
b1 <- unname(b["log_gdppc"])
b2 <- unname(b["log_gdppc2"])

# 2) Grid over requested range
ekc_shape_df <- tibble(
  x = seq(0, 20, length.out = 400)
) |>
  mutate(ekc_shape = b1 * x + b2 * x^2)

# 3) Delta-method SE for m(x) = b1*x + b2*x^2
V_sub <- V[c("log_gdppc", "log_gdppc2"), c("log_gdppc", "log_gdppc2")]

ekc_shape_df <- ekc_shape_df |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(
    se = sqrt(c(x, x^2) %*% V_sub %*% c(x, x^2)),
    lo = ekc_shape - 1.96 * se,
    hi = ekc_shape + 1.96 * se
  ) |>
  ungroup()

# 4) Turning point
x_star <- -b1 / (2 * b2)
gdppc_star <- exp(x_star)

# 5) Plot
ggplot(ekc_shape_df, aes(x = x, y = ekc_shape)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lo, ymax = hi), alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = x_star, linetype = "dashed") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 20), ylim = c(0, 13)) +
  labs(
    title = "Empirical EKC Shape from Two-way FE (Extended Model)",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Curve: β̂1 x + β̂2 x² (x = log GDP per capita). ",
      "95% CI via delta method (country-clustered). ",
      "\nTurning point at x* = ", round(x_star, 3),
      " (GDPpc* ≈ ", scales::comma(round(gdppc_star, 0)), ")."
    ),
    x = "log(GDP per capita)",
    y = expression(widehat(log(CO[2]))~~"(per capita)")
  )

title: 🧾📌 Capstone Project Guide
text:
In DANL 410: Data Analytics Capstone, you will complete an end-to-end analytics project—starting from a focused question, moving through data work and analysis, and ending with a polished report and presentation.
This course is designed to help you practice what data analysts actually do:

Translate a real-world problem into an analytics question
Find and evaluate data (or create a clean dataset)
Clean, transform, and explore the data
Apply an appropriate machine learning or statistical method (e.g., regression, classification, clustering)
Communicate findings clearly with visuals and writing
Publish your work on your Quarto website (GitHub Pages)







Note



You do not need the “perfect” topic at the beginning. Your goal early in the semester is to build a feasible plan with a clear question, a credible dataset, and a reasonable method.





Each team may have one or two members.
Every team member is expected to contribute actively and understand the entire project.
Teams should be formed by Wednesday, Feb 11, 11:59 P.M.
If your team has two members, a representative should email Prof. Choe at bchoe@geneseo.edu, cc-ing a team member.

title: 🧾📌 Capstone Project Guide
text:
🧾 Research Kick-off Report — Structure
Your Research Kick-off Report (2–3 pages) is a concise proposal that explains:

what you plan to study,
why it matters (business, social, or policy relevance),
how you will study it using data.

Include these sections:

Working Title & Topic

A clear, descriptive title.
2–4 sentences describing the topic and setting.

Research Question

State one main research question in a single sentence.
Add 1–3 sub-questions if helpful.

Motivation / Value

Why would someone care?
Who is the “stakeholder” (a business, customers, local government, nonprofit, campus office, etc.)?
What decision, insight, or understanding might the analysis support?

Data Plan

What dataset(s) will you use?
Unit of analysis (customer? county? day? product? student? transaction?)
Key variables you expect to use (inputs and outcomes)
Data limitations you already anticipate (missingness, small sample, measurement, bias)

Method Plan (Required: include one ML/statistical component)

What approach will you use and why?
Examples:

Regression (prediction or cause-and-effect)
Classification (e.g., churn / pass-fail / fraud / sentiment categories)
Clustering (e.g., segmentation)
Time-series forecasting (if appropriate)

What is your evaluation plan? (train/test split, accuracy/RMSE, cross-validation, or a clear validation idea)

References

Use a consistent citation style.
For online sources: include URL and date of access.








Note



💡 Think of the Kick-off Report as your project blueprint. You are not expected to have final results yet—but your plan should be credible and feasible.

title: 🧾📌 Capstone Project Guide
text:
🧭 Research Idea Logic
A strong capstone topic is:

Specific enough to answer within one semester

Data-supported (you can actually obtain usable data)

Method-feasible (you can execute and interpret a reasonable model)

Meaningful (it has a real stakeholder, decision, or insight)

Think in “question types”:








Type
Purpose
Example




Descriptive
Summarize what is happening
“How have housing prices changed across counties since 2018?”


Comparative
Compare groups or time periods
“Do outcomes differ by region, income level, or policy change?”


Predictive
Predict an outcome using features
“Can we predict churn using usage behavior?”


Segmentation
Identify clusters / groups
“Can we cluster customers into meaningful segments?”


Diagnostic
Identify drivers of an outcome
“Which factors are most associated with delays?”









Warning



Avoid questions that are too broad:

“How does social media affect society?”

Instead, narrow:

“Which post features predict higher engagement for a specific account/category?”





💾 Using Data Effectively
You are encouraged to use real-world data and present evidence using:

Clean tables (summary statistics, grouped means, counts)
Clear visualizations (distributions, trends, comparisons, relationships)
A method you can explain and justify
Transparent code and reproducible workflow

Guidelines:

Start with a dataset you can actually obtain quickly (Week 2–4).
Prefer data that has:

Enough observations to learn patterns
Clear variable definitions
A usable target/outcome variable (for prediction/classification), or a clear grouping structure (for clustering)

Use modeling only when it adds value:

A “fancy” model with weak interpretation is worse than a simple model well explained.

Always cite data sources and document data cleaning steps.







Tip



You don’t need complex modeling to do a strong capstone— but you do need clear evidence + clear reasoning + clear communication.
