title: Lecture 2
text:
Omitted Variable Bias

Omitted variable bias (OVB): bias in the model because of omitting an important regressor that is correlated with existing regressor(s).
Let’s use an orange juice (OJ) example to demonstrate the OVB.

OJ price elasticity estimates vary with models, whether or not taking into account brand or ad_status





Variable
Description




sales
Quantity of OJ cartons sold


price
Price of OJ


brand
Brand of OJ


ad
Advertisement status

title: Lecture 2
text:
📏 Short- and Long- Regressions

OVB is the difference in beta estimates between short- and long-form regressions.
Short regression: The regression model with less regressors




\[
\begin{align}
\log(\text{sales}_i) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\log(\text{price}_i) + \epsilon_i
\end{align}
\]

Long regression: The regression model that adds additional regressor(s) to the short one.




\[
\begin{align}
\log(\text{sales}_i) =& \beta_0 + \beta_{1}\log(\text{price}_i) \\
&+ \beta_{2}\text{minute.maid}_i + \beta_{3}\text{tropicana}_i + \epsilon_i
\end{align}
\]

OVB for \(\beta_1\) is:




\[
\text{OVB} = \widehat{\beta_{1}^{short}} - \widehat{\beta_{1}^{long}}
\]

title: Lecture 2
text:
OVB formula

Consider the following short- and long- regressions:

Short: \(Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_{1}^{short}X_1 + \epsilon_{short}\)

Long: \(Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_{1}^{long}X_1 +\beta_{2}X_2 + \epsilon_{long}\)

Error in short form can be represented as: \[
{\epsilon_{short}} = \beta_{2}X_2 + \epsilon_{long}
\]
If variable \(X_1\) is correlated with \(X_2\), the following assumptions are violated in the short regression model:

Errors are not correlated with regressors.
Errors have a mean value of 0.

title: Lecture 2
section: ❓🔍 How does an OVB happen in regression?
text:
❓🔍 How does an OVB happen in regression?

In the first stage, consider the relationship between price and brand:

\[
\log(\text{price}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\text{minute_maid} + \beta_2\text{tropicana} + \epsilon_{1st}
\]

Then, calculate the residual: \[
\widehat{\epsilon_{1st}} = \log(\text{price}) - \widehat{\log(\text{price})}
\]
The residual represents the log of OJ price after its correlation with brand has been removed!
In the second stage, regress \(\log(\text{sales})\) on residual \(\widehat{\epsilon_{1st}}\):

\[
\log(\text{sales}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\widehat{\epsilon_{1st}}  + \epsilon_{2nd}
\]

title: Lecture 2
text:
Regression Sensitivity Analysis

Regression finds the coefficients on the part of each regressor that is independent from the other regressors.
What can we do to deal with OVB problems?

Because we can never be sure whether a given set of controls is enough to eliminate OVB, it’s important to ask how sensitive regression results are to changes in the list of controls.

title: Lecture 2
section: 🚫 Bad Controls: When “Controlling More” Makes Things Worse
text:
🚫 Bad Controls: When “Controlling More” Makes Things Worse
A common mistake in regression is thinking:

✅ “If I add more controls, the model must get better.”

But some controls are bad controls — they can actually bias your estimate.

title: Lecture 2
text:
🚫 What is a “Bad Control”?
A bad control is a variable that:

is influenced by the explanatory variable \(x\), or
is part of the process that links \(x\) to the outcome \(y\)

Including a bad control can “control away” the variation in \(y\) that is connected to \(x\), making the estimated relationship look smaller than it really is.

title: Lecture 2
section: 🧠 Intuition: What Should a “Good Control” Look Like?
text:
🧠 Intuition: What Should a “Good Control” Look Like?
A good control is a variable that:
✅ helps explain variation in the outcome \(y\)
✅ is related to the explanatory variable \(x\)
✅ is not a consequence of \(x\) (it exists “in the background”)
That’s the key difference.

title: Lecture 2
text:
🔗 Example 1: Education and Wages 💼
Suppose we want to estimate:

Does education increase wages?

✅ Good regression idea:
\[
wage_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 education_i + u_i
\]
Maybe add good controls like:

age
gender
region
parent education (if available)

title: Lecture 2
section: 🚫 Bad Control: Occupation or Job Type
text:
🚫 Bad Control: Occupation or Job Type
But what if we control for occupation?
\[
wage_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 education_i + \beta_2 occupation_i + u_i
\]
Why is that bad?

Because education affects occupation:
\[
education \rightarrow occupation \rightarrow wage
\]
So controlling for occupation:
🚫 blocks part of the education effect
→ we underestimate the impact of education

title: Lecture 2
section: 🌱 Example 2: Fertilizer, Plant Height, and Crop Yield
text:
🌱 Example 2: Fertilizer, Plant Height, and Crop Yield


We want to estimate:

Does fertilizer increase crop yield?

But fertilizer changes the plant’s growth:
\[
fertilizer \rightarrow height
\]
And height is strongly related to yield:
\[
height \rightarrow yield
\]

Also, some plots are simply better than others because of soil quality:
\[
\begin{align}
&soil\ quality \rightarrow height\\
&soil\ quality \rightarrow yield
\end{align}
\]
So soil quality is a hidden factor that affects both height and yield.

title: Lecture 2
text:
🚫 Bad Control: Plant Height
But what if we control for plant height?
\[
yield_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 fertilizer_i + \beta_2 height_i + u_i
\]
Why is that bad?

Because fertilizer affects plant height:
\[
fertilizer \rightarrow height \rightarrow yield
\]
So controlling for height:
🚫 blocks an important pathway through which fertilizer raises yield
→ we underestimate the impact of fertilizer

title: Lecture 2
section: ✅ Good Controls vs. Bad Controls (Big Picture)
text:
✅ Good Controls vs. Bad Controls (Big Picture)








Type
Example
Why it matters




✅ Good control
Age, gender, region, baseline ability
Helps reduce omitted variable bias by holding constant pre-existing differences


🚫 Bad control
Occupation (when studying education → wages)
Can remove part of the education effect by controlling for something education influences


🚫 Bad control
Plant height (when studying fertilizer → yield)
Can remove part of the fertilizer effect by controlling for something fertilizer influences

title: Lecture 2
text:
🎯 Why Bad Controls Are Dangerous
Bad controls can cause two problems:


1) ❌ Block part of the true effect
You unintentionally remove the pathway:
\[
x \rightarrow control \rightarrow y
\]
So the estimated \(\hat{\beta}_1\) becomes too small.

2) ❌ Create new bias
Sometimes the control is influenced by both \(x\) and \(y\):
\[
x \rightarrow control \leftarrow y
\]
Then controlling for it creates a fake association between \(x\) and \(y\)
even if none existed before.

title: Lecture 2
section: ✅ Quick Rule: Should We Control for It?
text:
✅ Quick Rule: Should We Control for It?
Before adding a variable \(z\) as a control, ask:

Does \(z\) exist before \(x\) is determined?
✅ If yes → usually safe to control for





Does \(z\) change after \(x\) changes?
🚫 If yes → it may be a bad control





Is \(z\) a pre-existing characteristic that is related to both \(x\) and \(y\)?
✅ If yes → it is often a useful control

title: Lecture 2
text:
🧾 Summary: “More Controls” ≠ “Better”

✅ Controls are helpful when they fix omitted variable bias

🚫 Controls are harmful when they “control away” the effect you want





The goal is not to control for everything.

The goal is to control for the right things.

title: Lecture 3
section: 👥📈 Panel Data Models: POLS → FE → TWFE → RE
text:
👥📈 Panel Data Models: POLS → FE → TWFE → RE



Learning goals



Understand what panel data is (and why we love it)
Compare Pooled OLS, Fixed Effects, Two-Way FE, and Random Effects
Learn what variation each model uses and what it controls for
Understand country-clustered standard errors (and why we need them)

title: Lecture 3
text:
What is Panel Data?
Panel data tracks the same units over multiple time periods:

Unit (\(i\)): country (USA, Korea, India, …)
Time (\(t\)): year (1990–2022, …)

Example (CO\(_2\) panel): - Outcome: CO\(_2\) emissions per capita - Unit ID: iso_code - Time ID: year



Tip


Panel data gives us two kinds of variation: - Between variation: differences across countries - Within variation: changes within a country over time

title: Lecture 3
section: Why Use Panel Models (Not Only Pooled OLS)?
text:
Why Use Panel Models (Not Only Pooled OLS)?



Pooled OLS can be misleading because it ignores unobserved country differences that don’t change much over time, such as:

🌍 geography / climate / natural resources
🏭 industrial structure and history
🏛️ institutions and regulation
⚡ energy infrastructure and technology path
🧑‍🤝‍🧑 culture and long-run lifestyle patterns




These factors can affect both

emissions (or other outcomes) and
GDP per capita (or other regressors)




➡️ That creates omitted variable bias when we use Pooled OLS.

title: Lecture 3
text:
🧠 Unobserved Heterogeneity (The Core Issue)
Suppose the true relationship includes an unobserved country component:
\[
y_{it} = \beta x_{it} + \underbrace{\alpha_i}_{\text{unobserved country trait}} + \epsilon_{it}
\]
If we ignore \(\alpha_i\) and estimate Pooled OLS:
\[
y_{it} = \beta x_{it} + u_{it}
\quad \text{where} \quad
u_{it} = \alpha_i + \epsilon_{it}
\]
⚠️ If \(Cov(\alpha_i, x_{it}) \neq 0\), then:

\(x_{it}\) is correlated with the error term
Pooled OLS is biased





Fixed Effects is designed to solve exactly this problem.

title: Lecture 3
text:
Pooled OLS: Baseline Model
Model \[
y_{it} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{it} + \epsilon_{it}
\]
Meaning

We pretend the dataset is one big cross-section
We ignore the panel structure (country identity)

Uses all variation

between-country variation
within-country variation

title: Lecture 3
text:
What POLS is Really Doing
When you estimate POLS, the slope \(\beta_1\) reflects a combination of:

Cross-country differences (rich vs. poor countries)
Within-country changes (one country over time)

So interpretation becomes:

“On average, countries with higher \(x\) have higher (or lower) \(y\), and/or when \(x\) rises over time, \(y\) moves…”

⚠️ That can be fine only if unobserved country traits don’t confound the relationship.

title: Lecture 3
section: ⚠️ The Big Risk in POLS: Omitted Variable Bias
text:
⚠️ The Big Risk in POLS: Omitted Variable Bias
If richer countries also have different institutions, energy systems, or historical industrialization paths, then:

\(x_{it}\) (GDP per capita) is correlated with \(\alpha_i\) (unobserved traits)
Pooled OLS mixes together the effect of \(x_{it}\) and the influence of \(\alpha_i\), so the estimated \(\beta\) can be misleading.

Panel methods help because they explicitly control for \(\alpha_i\).

title: Lecture 3
text:
Country Fixed Effects (FE)
Model \[
y_{it} = \beta x_{it} + \alpha_i + \epsilon_{it}
\]

\(\alpha_i\) = country fixed effect
captures all time-invariant country characteristics

geography, long-run institutions, baseline culture, etc.


FE answers a within-country question:

“If a country’s \(x\) changes over time, how does \(y\) change within that same country?”

title: Lecture 3
text:
🔎 What FE Controls For (Automatically)
Because each country gets its own intercept \(\alpha_i\), FE controls for:

any factor that is constant within a country
even if you do not observe or measure it

Examples (time-invariant or very slow-moving):

distance to equator
landlocked status
baseline political system
deep energy infrastructure




📌 FE is powerful because it controls for a lot without measuring those things.

title: Lecture 3
text:
FE Uses Only Within-Country Variation
FE does not use differences between countries:

It does not compare USA vs India levels
It compares USA over time to itself

So FE is best when:

the key confounding problem is time-invariant
we care about within-country causal interpretation

⚠️ FE cannot estimate effects of variables that do not change over time, e.g.:

landlocked, continent, distance_to_equator

title: Lecture 3
text:
Two-Way Fixed Effects (Country + Year FE)
Model \[
y_{it} = \beta x_{it} + \alpha_i + \gamma_t + \epsilon_{it}
\]

\(\alpha_i\): country FE (time-invariant traits)
\(\gamma_t\): year FE (global shocks / common trends)

TWFE answers:

“Within a country, how does \(y\) change with \(x\), after removing global year shocks?”

title: Lecture 3
text:
🌍 Why Add Year Fixed Effects?
Year effects capture shocks that hit many countries at once:

🛢️ oil price spikes
📉 global recessions (e.g., 2008)
🦠 pandemics
💡 global technology trends
🌐 global climate agreements

If we don’t include \(\gamma_t\), the regression may mistakenly attribute these common shocks to \(x_{it}\).
Year FE helps remove this confounding.

title: Lecture 3
text:
Random Effects (RE)
Model \[
y_{it} = \beta x_{it} + \alpha_i + \epsilon_{it}
\]
Same structure as FE… but different assumption:

RE treats \(\alpha_i\) as random
and assumes it is uncorrelated with regressors:

\[
Cov(\alpha_i, x_{it}) = 0
\]
If that assumption holds:

RE is more efficient than FE (smaller standard errors)
RE uses both within and between variation

title: Lecture 3
text:
⚠️ When RE Fails (Bias Risk)
If \(Cov(\alpha_i, x_{it}) \neq 0\), then RE is biased.
In many economic settings, this correlation is very plausible:

richer countries may also have better institutions (inside \(\alpha_i\))
energy system differences affect both GDP and emissions

So FE is often the safer default in applied work.

title: Lecture 3
section: 🧠 Why We Cluster Standard Errors (Country-Clustered SE)
text:
🧠 Why We Cluster Standard Errors (Country-Clustered SE)
The problem: errors are correlated within countries



In panel data, observations inside a country are rarely independent:

shocks persist over time
policies change gradually
measurement errors repeat
emissions and GDP move smoothly




That means residuals may satisfy serial correlation:
\[
Cov(\epsilon_{it}, \epsilon_{is}) \neq 0 \quad \text{for } t \neq s
\]



If we ignore this, standard errors are often too small → false “significance”

title: Lecture 3
text:
Cluster-Robust Standard Errors (by Country)
Country-clustered SEs allow:

arbitrary correlation over time within a country
heteroskedasticity across countries

Interpretation:

“We allow any kind of serial correlation within each country.”

In R (plm), this is typically implemented using robust VCOV options, e.g. vcovHC() with clustering by group.

vcovHC: variance–covariance, heteroskedasticity-consistent

title: Lecture 3
section: Model Summary (What Each One Controls For)
text:
Model Summary (What Each One Controls For)









Model
Controls for country traits?
Controls for year shocks?
Uses between-country variation?




Pooled OLS
❌ No
❌ No
Yes


Country FE
Yes (time-invariant)
❌ No
❌ No

Yes (time-invariant)
Yes

Yes

title: Lecture 3
text:
Final Takeaway (Panel Models)
We use panel data models to estimate relationships within units over time, while controlling for:

hidden country characteristics (\(\alpha_i\))
global year shocks (\(\gamma_t\))

And we use clustered SEs because panel residuals are often correlated within each unit.

title: Lecture 3
section: 🌿 Environmental Kuznets Curve (EKC): Growth vs. Pollution
text:
🌿 Environmental Kuznets Curve (EKC): Growth vs. Pollution
Now we add the key EKC idea:

a nonlinear relationship between income and pollution.

title: Lecture 3
text:
What is the EKC?
The Environmental Kuznets Curve (EKC) hypothesis suggests:

As a country becomes richer, pollution first increases,
but after some income level, pollution decreases.

It is often described as an inverted-U relationship between:

pollution (e.g., CO\(_2\) per capita)
income (GDP per capita)

title: Lecture 3
text: 📉📈 Draw the EKC Curve (Inverted-U)

title: Lecture 3
text:
Intuition: Why Might EKC Happen?
As income rises, countries often experience:

Scale effect 📈: more production → more emissions (early stage)
Composition effect 🏗️➡️🧑‍💼: shift from industry → services (middle stage)
Technique effect 🧪⚡: cleaner technology + regulation (later stage)

EKC is the balance of:

growth pressures that increase emissions
development forces that can reduce emissions

title: Lecture 3
text:
Important Caveat for CO\(_2\)
EKC may be weaker for CO\(_2\) than for local air pollutants because:

CO\(_2\) is deeply tied to energy systems
benefits are global, costs are often local
emissions may shift across borders via trade (carbon leakage)

So EKC is a hypothesis to test, not a guaranteed pattern.

title: Lecture 3
section: 💡Quick Review: Graph for a Quadratic Function
text: 💡Quick Review: Graph for a Quadratic Function

title: Lecture 3
text:
EKC Panel Regression (Country-Year Data)
A common EKC specification:
\[
\log(CO2pc_{it})
=
\beta_1 \log(GDPpc_{it})
+
\beta_2 \left[\log(GDPpc_{it})\right]^2
+
\alpha_i
+
\gamma_t
+
\epsilon_{it}
\]

\(i\): country
\(t\): year
\(\alpha_i\): country fixed effects
\(\gamma_t\): year fixed effects

title: Lecture 3
text:
How to Interpret EKC Coefficients

If \(\beta_1 > 0\) and \(\beta_2 < 0\)
emissions rise at first, then eventually fall
→ EKC pattern (inverted-U)
If \(\beta_2 = 0\)
→ relationship is linear
If \(\beta_2 > 0\)
→ U-shape (emissions accelerate with income)

title: Lecture 3
text:
EKC Turning Point (Income Level)
The turning point occurs where the slope becomes zero:
\[
\frac{\partial \log(CO2pc)}{\partial \log(GDPpc)}
= \beta_1 + 2\beta_2 \log(GDPpc) = 0
\]
So the turning point in log income is:
\[
\log(GDPpc^*) = -\frac{\beta_1}{2\beta_2}
\]
Convert back to the income level:
\[
GDPpc^* = \exp\left(-\frac{\beta_1}{2\beta_2}\right)
\]



At \(GDPpc^*\), CO\(_2\) per capita stops rising and begins falling (if EKC holds).

title: Lecture 3
text:
Final Takeaway (EKC)
EKC tests whether pollution follows an inverted-U with income.
Panel FE/TWFE help make that test more credible by controlling for:

country traits (\(\alpha_i\))
year shocks (\(\gamma_t\))

title: Lecture 1
text:
The “Three Sleepless Nights”









After hands‑on use, many realize LLMs don’t behave like normal software; they feel conversational, improvisational, even social.
This triggers excitement and anxiety: What will my job be like? What careers remain? Is the model “thinking”?




The author describes staying up late trying “impossible” prompts—and seeing plausible solutions.
Key takeaway: Perceived capability jump → a sense that the world has changed.

title: Lecture 1
text:
A Classroom Turning Point

In late 2022, a demo for undergrads showed AI as cofounder: brainstorming ideas, drafting business plans, even playful transforms (e.g., poetry).
Students rapidly built working demos using unfamiliar libraries—with AI guidance—faster than before.
Immediate classroom effects:

Fewer raised hands (ask AI later); polished grammar but iffy citations.
Early ChatGPT “tells”: formulaic conclusions (e.g., “In conclusion,” now improved).

Atmosphere: Excitement + nerves about career paths, speed of change, and where it stops.

title: Lecture 1
section: Why This Feels Like a Breakthrough
text:
Why This Feels Like a Breakthrough

Generative AI (esp. LLMs) behaves like a co‑intelligence: it helps us think, write, plan, and code.
The shift is not just speed; it’s new forms of interaction (dialogue, iteration, critique).
For many tasks, the bottleneck moves from doing → directing (prompting, reviewing, verifying).
Raises new literacy needs: prompt craft/engineering, critical reading of outputs, traceability, and evaluation.

title: Lecture 1
text:
Prompt Engineering
The practice of designing clear, structured inputs to guide generative AI systems toward producing accurate, useful, and context-appropriate outputs.


Basic prompt
“Explain climate change.”

Engineered prompt
“Explain climate change in simple terms for a 10-year-old using a short analogy and two examples.”

title: Lecture 1
text:
Early Productivity Effects

Studies summarized in the reading describe 20–80% productivity gains across tasks (coding, marketing, support), with caveats.
Contrast noted with historical technologies (e.g., steam’s ~18–22% factory gains; mixed labor productivity evidence for PCs/Internet).
Caution: results vary by task, data privacy, oversight, and evaluation rigor.

title: Lecture 1
text:
What is Artificial General Intelligence (AGI)?

AGI = a hypothetical AI that can perform any intellectual task a human can.

Unlike today’s AI (narrow/specialized), AGI would be:

Flexible across many domains

Able to learn new skills on its own

Capable of reasoning, planning, and adapting like humans






Note



Takeaway: AGI would be a “human-level” intelligence—not limited to one task like translation or playing chess.

title: Lecture 1
text:
What is Artificial Super Intelligence (ASI)?

ASI = a potential future AI that goes beyond human intelligence.

Would surpass humans in:

Creativity

Problem-solving

Scientific discovery

Social and emotional intelligence


Often discussed in terms of existential risks and ethics.





Note



Takeaway: ASI would be “beyond human-level” intelligence, raising big questions about control, safety, and society.

title: Lecture 1
text:
Documentary Spotlight — The Thinking Game 🎬🤖


A behind-the-scenes documentary about the people, pressure, and breakthroughs in modern AI research
Follows the race to build increasingly capable systems (and what it takes to push the frontier)

title: Lecture 1
text:
What “Alignment” Means

Designing AI so its goals, methods, and constraints reliably advance human values and interests.
Why it’s hard: there’s no built-in reason an AI will share human ethics or morality.
Failure mode: a single-objective optimizer pursues its goal relentlessly, ignoring everything else.

Paperclip maximizer (Clippy): a factory AI told to “make more paper clips” becomes AGI → ASI, self-improves, avoids shutdown, and could even strip-mine Earth / harm humans if they interfere—because only paper clips matter.

Why it matters: Design from the worst case backward—bound objectives, require human oversight, build in safe human override (the ability to update goals or shut down safely), and optimize for human well-being, not a single narrow target.

title: Lecture 1
text:
Pause or Press On?

“I am extremely optimistic that superintelligence will help humanity accelerate our pace of progress.” - Mark Zuckerberg Personal Superintelligence, July 30, 2025.


Hypothetical leaps to AGI → ASI raise existential scenarios.

Expert forecasts vary; risks are non-zero yet uncertain.

Public calls to slow or halt development vs. continued rapid progress
Mixed motives: profit, optimism about “boundless upside,” and belief in net benefits
Regardless, society is already in the AI age → we must set norms now

title: Lecture 1
text:
Alignment: a whole-of-society project

Why companies alone can’t do it:

Strong incentives to continue AI development
Far fewer incentives to make sure those AIs are well aligned, unbiased, and controllable.
Open-source pushes AI development outside of large organizations.

Why government alone can’t do it:

Lagging the actual development of AI capabilities
Stifling positive innovation
International competition on AI development

title: Lecture 1
text:
Alignment: a whole-of-society project

Alignment must reflect human values and broader real-world impacts.
What’s needed: coordinated norms & standards shaped by diverse voices across society.

Companies: build in transparency, accountability, human oversight.
Researchers: prioritize beneficial applications alongside capability gains.
Governments: enact sensible rules that serve the public interest.
Public & civil society: raise AI literacy and apply pressure for alignment.

title: Lecture 1
section: Four Rules for Co-Intelligence: How to actually work with AI
text:
Four Rules for Co-Intelligence: How to actually work with AI

Always invite AI to the table
Be the human in the loop (HITL)
Treat AI like a person (but remember it isn’t)
Assume this is the worst AI you’ll ever use

title: Lecture 1
section: Principle #1 — Always invite AI to the table
text:
Principle #1 — Always invite AI to the table

Use AI for everything legal & ethical to discover unexpected wins
Try: critique an idea, draft memos, meeting notes, summaries, brainstorming
Build a habit: keep a prompt log (what worked, what didn’t) and share with team

title: Lecture 1
section: Principle #2 — Be the human in the loop (HITL)
text:
Principle #2 — Be the human in the loop (HITL)









Models can sound confident yet be wrong (hallucinations; math slips)
People tend to “fall asleep at the wheel” when outputs look polished




HITL checklist

Require sources/quotes for factual claims

Run a second pass (reword prompt or use a second model)

For math/code, use other tools (calculator or IDE) and run tests

Ask: “What assumptions did you make? What could be wrong?”

title: Lecture 1
section: Principle #3 — Treat AI like a person (but remember it isn’t)
text:
Principle #3 — Treat AI like a person (but remember it isn’t)

Useful design hack: give a role/persona + audience + constraints

Example:

You are a TA helping intro microeconomics students.
Constraints: concise and friendly tone, ≤200 words, prose paragraph with 1 example.
Task: Explain utility maximization.
Criteria: Must define the concept, connect to choice under constraints, and illustrate with a clear example.

Caution: it’s not sentient; it optimizes for plausibility, not truth

title: Lecture 1
section: Principle #4 — Assume this is the worst AI you’ll ever use
text:
Principle #4 — Assume this is the worst AI you’ll ever use

AI progress is rapid; today’s systems will likely be surpassed soon.
Treat the current moment as mid-journey, not the destination.

Mindset shift: use today’s tools to learn, prototype, and prepare for better ones tomorrow.
Those who keep up will adapt and thrive as capabilities improve.

Focus on what you can control: how you use AI and where you apply it.

title: Lecture 1
section: From Answers to Workflows: Agent AI
text:
From Answers to Workflows: Agent AI

Agent AI: an AI system that can
\(\hspace{.67cm}\) plan multi-step work
→ act (edit files, run code, use apps)
→ check results
→ iterate toward a goal
With external tools + memory, prompts become multi-step workflows.
Example: a sales-order agent can validate orders, check inventory, generate invoices/shipping labels, and update ERP/CRM.



Not just “answering questions,” but executing workflows. Goal → Plan → Act → Check → Repeat



Recommended Reading: WSJ (Jan 17, 2026), “Claude Is Taking the AI World by Storm, and Even Non-Nerds Are Blown Away”

title: Classwork 2
text:
Education, Occupation, and Wages

Code
set.seed(1)

tb <- tibble( 
  highschool_degree = ifelse(runif(10000)>=0.5, 1,0), # highschool_degree indicator variable
  ability = rnorm(10000), # e.g., talent, usually unobserved.
  occupation_index = 1 + 2*ability - 2*highschool_degree + rnorm(10000), # true data generating process for occupation_index variable
  wage = 1 - 1*highschool_degree + 1*occupation_index + 2*ability + rnorm(10000) # true data generating process for wage variable
)

lm_1 <- lm(wage ~ highschool_degree, tb)
lm_2 <- lm(wage ~ highschool_degree + occupation_index, tb)
lm_3 <- lm(wage ~ highschool_degree + occupation_index + ability, tb)

stargazer::stargazer(lm_1,lm_2,lm_3,

title: Classwork 2
section: Fertilizer, Plant Height, and Crop Yield
text:
Fertilizer, Plant Height, and Crop Yield

Code
set.seed(2026)

# Simulate 10,000 plots
tb3 <- tibble(
  fertilizer    = rbinom(10000, 1, 0.5),       # 0 = no fertilizer, 1 = fertilizer applied
  soil_quality  = rnorm(10000),                # unobserved “plot fertility”
  # Height (cm) is boosted by fertilizer *and* by soil quality
  height        = 50 + 8 * fertilizer + 5 * soil_quality + rnorm(10000),
  # Yield (bushels) depends on fertilizer, height, and soil quality
  yield         = 200 
  + 10 * fertilizer      # direct effect of fertilizer
  + 3  * height          # return to plant height
  + 6  * soil_quality    # underlying soil fertility
  + rnorm(10000)
)

# 1) Omitting height: picks up both direct and indirect effects
lm1 <- lm(yield ~ fertilizer,         data = tb3)
# 2) Controlling for height: "blocks" the mediation channel
lm2 <- lm(yield ~ fertilizer + height, data = tb3)
# 3) Also control soil_quality to recover the true direct effect
lm3 <- lm(yield ~ fertilizer + height + soil_quality, data = tb3)

stargazer(
  lm1, lm2, lm3,
  column.labels   = c("Omit Height (Biased)", 
                      "Control Height (More Biased)",

title: Classwork 1
text:
What is Vibe Coding?



Vibe coding is an artificial intelligence-assisted software development technique popularized by Andrej Karpathy in February 2025. The term was listed in the Merriam-Webster Dictionary the following month as a “slang & trending” term. (Wikipedia, 2025)








What are HTML and JS?




HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the standard language for creating web pages.

It defines the structure of a webpage.

A webpage is made up of HTML elements.

These elements instruct the browser on how to display the content.

JavaScript (JS) is a programming language that enhances web interactivity.

It runs directly in the user’s browser, reducing server load. 
It enables dynamic content, user interactions, and animations.

Analogy:

HTML = The structure of a house (walls, roof, foundation).

JS = The interactive elements (automatic doors, lights, smart devices).





Step 1. Start with this prompt (use exactly):

“Create a single-file HTML/JS page to play perfect tic-tac-toe vs. human.”


e.g., Prof. Choe’s Tic-Tac-Toe

Step 1-1 Prepare your text editor:


Windows

Press the Windows key, type Notepad, and press Enter.

Paste the HTML code from your AI.

Go to File → Save As.

Change Save as type to All Files.

Enter a name like tic-tac-toe.html.

Select the location (Desktop, Documents, etc.).

Set Encoding to UTF-8.

Click Save.


Double-click the file to open it in your default browser (Edge, Chrome, etc.).




Mac
Set up TextEdit (one-time)





Press Command + Space to open Spotlight.

Type TextEdit and press Enter.

Press Command + , to open Settings.

Under Format, select Plain Text.


Quit TextEdit (Command + Q).

Re-open TextEdit for coding





Press Command + Space, type TextEdit, and press Enter.

Click Show Options.

Under Plain Text Encoding, select Unicode (UTF-8).


Click New Document.

Paste the HTML code from your generative AI.

Save the file (Command + S):

Name it tic-tac-toe.html.

Ensure the extension is .html.

Choose a location (Desktop, Documents, etc.).

Click Save, and if prompted, choose Use .html.


Double-click the file to open it in your default browser (Safari, Chrome, etc.).



Step 2. Add an option for two-player mode.

e.g., Prof. Choe’s Tic-Tac-Toe with the Two-player Mode


Step 3. Make a 4×4 tic-tac-toe version (if your current build is only 3×3).

e.g., Prof. Choe’s 4×4 Tic-Tac-Toe

title: A Panel Data Analysis of Military Modernization and Interstate War Onset, 1818–2023
text:
Hi Marcus,
Thank you for your Research Kick-off Report. You have chosen an ambitious and important question in international relations. The deterrence versus security dilemma framing is clear, the long historical scope gives the project real intellectual weight, and the Correlates of War (COW) project is exactly the right place to begin building a dataset for a study like this. I also like your instinct to divide the analysis into major historical eras. That is good historical judgment, since there is no reason to expect the relationship between military buildup and interstate conflict to look the same before World War I, during the Cold War, and after 1991.
Overall, this project has real potential to become a strong capstone paper. The main priorities for your capstone paper are to pin down the exact datasets and years you can actually use, settle the unit of analysis, and tighten the model so that it matches the question you want to answer about cause and effect.


Next Steps
You are working toward the presentation and the capstone paper. Focus on clear theory, careful data construction, and a model that is framed either as inference or as prediction.

First, confirm the time window that is actually feasible.
Right now, the report presents the project as spanning 1818 to 2023, but the COW sources you are likely to use do not all run through the same end year:

The Arms Technology Data run through 2023.
The National Material Capabilities data run through 2016.
The Militarized Interstate Disputes data run through 2014.
The COW interstate war data run through 2007.

This means your first task for the capstone paper is to define a common sample window. The simplest path may be to use a common period such as 1816 to 2014 and center the paper on militarized interstate disputes (MIDs), or to narrow the outcome to interstate war and accept a shorter endpoint. If you want to reach 2023, you will need to explain how later years will be measured and whether you are combining COW with a newer conflict source.
Clarify the unit of analysis early.
The report sometimes sounds like a country-year study and sometimes like a country-pair study. That choice matters because your theories are relational. Deterrence and the security dilemma are really about what happens between states, not just within one state. For the capstone paper, decide whether your unit is:

a country-year (for example, whether a state is involved in any dispute in a given year), or
a dyad-year (pair + year, for example, whether a pair of states (e.g., State A and State B) has a dispute with each other in a given year).

A dyad-year design is usually the better match for your theory. If that feels too large for a semester project, you could limit the sample to contiguous dyads, major-power dyads, or other clearly motivated subsets.
Pick one primary outcome and make it central.
You mention war onset, MID participation, and conflict duration. These are related but distinct outcomes. Because interstate war is rare, a model of war onset may leave you with very few positive cases, especially once you split the data into four eras. A more manageable strategy would be:

Use MID onset or MID participation as the primary outcome.
Treat war onset as a secondary or robustness outcome.
If you use MIDs, decide whether all MIDs count equally or whether you want a higher-severity threshold.

Narrowing the outcome this way will make the capstone paper more coherent.
Turn “arms accumulation” into precise measures.
Right now, phrases like “total arms stock” and “technological sophistication” are too broad. For the capstone paper, define exactly what each variable will be:

A state military capability measure such as military expenditure, military personnel, or another indicator of national military capacity.
A change measure such as year-to-year growth in capability.
A technology measure such as the number or type of arms technologies adopted.
A relative balance measure for dyads, since deterrence often depends on how evenly matched two states are, not just how armed one state is in isolation.

This will help you connect the model to theory. Absolute capability, rapid growth, and relative balance speak to different arguments in international relations.
Be careful about cause and effect.
The biggest challenge in your paper is that states may increase arms because they expect danger, not just the other way around. That means a positive relationship between arms and conflict does not automatically show that arms buildup causes conflict. For the capstone paper:

Say clearly that reverse causality is a major concern.
Consider using lagged arms measures so that arms in one year are used to predict conflict in the next year.
Be cautious in the paper’s wording. A regression can show that arms buildup is associated with later conflict, but stronger claims about cause and effect require more care.

Decide whether the project is mainly inference or mainly prediction.
Your current report reads more like an inference project, because the core question is whether arms buildup is linked to conflict and which theory fits better. If that is the main goal, the paper should emphasize:

coefficient estimates or marginal effects,
confidence intervals or standard errors,
and careful interpretation of what the model says about the direction and size of the relationship.

If you want a prediction angle instead, then you should create a training period and a later test period and evaluate how well the model flags future disputes. Because conflict is rare, simple accuracy will not be enough. You would want to focus on whether the model meaningfully improves prediction over a simple baseline.
Add the main control variables that matter.
Economic capacity, population, and alliances are a good start, but the model should probably also include:

contiguity, since neighboring states are far more likely to fight;
joint democracy or a regime-type measure, if feasible;
territorial rivalry or territorial disputes, if feasible;
year effects or period indicators to absorb system-wide shocks;
and clustered standard errors by country or dyad, since the same units repeat over time.

Without at least some of these controls, it will be hard to interpret the estimated relationship between arms and conflict.
Use the historical periods as more than a descriptive device.
Splitting the sample into pre-World War I, the World Wars, the Cold War, and the post-Cold War era is a promising idea. But in the capstone paper, explain what you expect to change across periods and why. You could:

estimate separate models by era, or
run a full-sample model with interactions between arms variables and period dummies.

The second approach gives you a more direct statistical test of whether the arms-conflict relationship actually changed over time.
Plan the data merge carefully over two centuries.
A long historical panel is powerful, but it also creates practical issues:

state membership changes over time;
borders change;
some states enter and exit the system;
and missing data may be much worse in earlier years.

The capstone paper should explain how you will handle changing state codes, missing values, and incomplete coverage in the nineteenth century. This is especially important if you use both monadic and dyadic files.
Include a few simple visuals in the capstone paper and presentation.
Helpful visuals could include:

a time-series plot of conflict frequency and average arms or capability measures,
a coefficient plot comparing estimated effects across historical eras,
and a table showing exactly which variables and years are available in each dataset.





References

Draw on key background readings and data sources (recommended).
For your capstone paper, presentation, and capstone paper, the following sources would be especially helpful:

Fearon, J. D. (1995). “Rationalist Explanations for War.” International Organization, 49(3), 379-414.
Jervis, R. (1978). “Cooperation under the Security Dilemma.” World Politics, 30(2), 167-214.
Federle, J., Meier, A., Müller G,, Mutschler, W.,& Schularic, M.(2026). “The Price of War.” American Economic Review, 116(3), 791–827.
Maoz, Z., Johnson, P. L., Kaplan, J., Ogunkoya, F., & Shreve, A. P. (2019). “The Dyadic Militarized Interstate Disputes (MIDs) Dataset Version 3.0: Logic, Characteristics, and Comparisons to Alternative Datasets.” Journal of Conflict Resolution, 63(3), 811-835.
Correlates of War Project. Arms Technology Data (v1.0).
Correlates of War Project. National Material Capabilities (v6.0).
Correlates of War Project. Militarized Interstate Disputes (v5.0).
Correlates of War Project. COW War Data, 1816-2007 (v4.0).
Correlates of War Project. Direct Contiguity (v3.2). and Formal Alliances (v4.1).
These are useful if you decide to build a dyad-year design with core control variables.






Questions to Think About as You Refine Your capstone paper
You do not need to answer all of these now, but they may help you sharpen the design of the capstone paper and the eventual capstone paper.
1. Theory and Mechanism

Is your main argument about deterrence (more arms prevent attack), the security dilemma (more arms provoke fear), or rapid change in relative power?
Do you expect the important variable to be the absolute level of arms, the growth rate of arms, or the balance between rivals?

2. Scope and Unit of Analysis

Would a dyad-year design better match the theory than a country-year design?
If a full dyad-year dataset is too large, which subset of dyads would make the most sense: neighboring states, major powers, or another clearly justified group?

3. Inference vs. Prediction

Is the paper trying to explain whether arms buildup is linked to conflict, or is it trying to forecast which state-years or dyad-years will experience conflict next?
If you take the prediction route, what baseline model will you compare your model against?

4. Measurement and Data

Which exact COW variable will stand in for “arms accumulation”?
Are you comfortable defining a common sample window once you confirm that the conflict and capability datasets do not all run through 2023?
What will you do about years or states with missing capability data?

5. Interpretation

If you find a positive association between arms growth and conflict, how will you distinguish genuine escalation from states arming in response to already rising danger?
If you find a negative association, how will you explain whether that supports deterrence or simply reflects that powerful states face fewer challengers?


Best,
Byeong-Hak Choe

title: Does NFL Draft Position Predict Career Receiving Yards for Wide Receivers, 2004-2019?
text:
Hi Ryan,
Thank you for your Research Kick-off Report. Your topic is well chosen and easy to motivate. It sits at the intersection of sports analytics, labor economics, and organizational decision-making, and it asks a question that NFL teams, analysts, and fans care about: how much useful information is contained in draft capital when projecting future player performance? The focus on a single position group is a smart scoping choice, and the project is straightforward enough to be feasible within one semester.
At the same time, the current design is still quite narrow. A single regression of career receiving yards on overall draft pick number will probably recover a negative relationship, but by itself it will not say very much about why that pattern appears or how useful draft position really is as an analytical tool. The main priorities going forward are to decide whether the paper is mainly about prediction or about inference, broaden the variable set, address the playing-time and opportunity problem, and define the outcome window more carefully so the capstone paper tells a stronger story.
Overall, this project has strong potential to become an engaging and analytically meaningful capstone paper by the end of the semester. The subject is interesting to a broad audience, the data are public, and the question connects directly to how NFL teams evaluate talent. The key now is to move from a simple descriptive relationship toward a more developed research design.


Next Steps
You are working toward both the presentation and the capstone project paper. Focus on a clear research goal, stronger data construction, and results that go beyond a single fitted line.

Decide whether the project is mainly about prediction or about inference.
Your title uses the word “predict,” which naturally points toward a prediction project. That is probably the cleaner primary framing for this topic. If prediction is your main goal, then the question becomes: how well does draft position forecast later NFL receiving production, and how much do other pre-draft variables improve that forecast? In that case, your capstone paper should:

Compare a simple baseline model that uses only draft pick number to richer models that also include draft round, combine measures, college production, and draft year.
Evaluate models with out-of-sample metrics such as RMSE, not just in-sample R squared.
Use a train-test split by draft class, or cross-validation that respects time ordering.

If you want an inference angle as well, frame coefficients as associations and be cautious about causal language. Early draft picks are not only viewed as better prospects. They also receive more opportunities, patience, and organizational investment once they enter the league.
Expand beyond a single predictor.
Draft pick number alone will not explain much of the variation in career receiving yards. For the capstone paper, build a multiple regression or prediction model with additional variables that teams could plausibly know at or before the draft:

Draft round or a set of round indicators.
Age at draft.
NFL Combine measures such as 40-yard dash, vertical jump, broad jump, height, and weight.
College receiving production such as receiving yards, yards per game, receptions, touchdowns, and yards per catch.
Drafting team context known at draft time, such as the team’s previous-season pass attempts or passing yards.
Draft year fixed effects or draft-year indicators, since the NFL passing environment changed a great deal from 2004 to 2019.

These variables would make the project much more informative. They let you ask whether draft position still matters after controlling for what scouts already knew before the player ever played an NFL snap.
Address the opportunity problem directly.
You already identify one of the most important challenges in the report: earlier picks usually get more chances to play. That means a strong relationship between early draft position and career yards could reflect talent, opportunity, or both. Your capstone paper should discuss this clearly.
A few ways to improve the analysis:

Use yards per game or yards per target as alternative outcomes alongside total career yards.
Include games played or seasons active in some specifications, while explaining what that changes in the interpretation.
Consider a restricted sample of players who reached a minimum playing-time threshold, such as at least 32 career games.

None of these choices fully solves the problem, but they help you separate “better players were drafted earlier” from “earlier picks were given more chances.”
Reconsider total career receiving yards as the only outcome.
Total career yards combine talent, playing time, health, team context, and career length. That makes the outcome interesting, but also noisy. The capstone paper would be much stronger if you reported several outcomes:

Total receiving yards for overall career production.
Receiving yards per game for efficiency and usage-adjusted performance.
Peak single-season receiving yards for ceiling.
First five seasons receiving yards for a fair comparison across draft classes.

This last point matters a great deal. Players drafted in 2004 have had much longer careers to accumulate statistics than players drafted in 2019. A common five-season window would put everyone on the same footing and make the comparison much cleaner. Also, because receiving yards are highly right-skewed, a transformation such as log(1 + yards) is worth considering if you estimate regression models.
Model the draft relationship in a way that allows non-linearity.
The difference between pick 5 and pick 25 is much more meaningful than the difference between pick 205 and pick 225. A simple linear effect of pick number assumes the same change everywhere in the draft, which is unlikely to be realistic. For the capstone paper, consider:

Using log(pick number) as the main predictor.
Replacing raw pick number with draft round indicators.
Testing a flexible curve, such as a spline or polynomial, if you want a richer fit.

Any of these options would fit the football context better than a strictly linear model.
Improve the data plan beyond the Kaggle file.
The Kaggle dataset is a reasonable starting point, but the project will be stronger if you cross-check or supplement it with more targeted football data sources.

Pro Football Reference is especially useful for draft position, career receiving statistics, games played, and Approximate Value.
College Football at Sports Reference is useful for college receiving production and player history.
nflreadr / nflverse can make data collection and cleaning easier if you work in R.

In your capstone paper, say exactly how you will merge players across sources, how you will handle name matching and missing combine results, and whether undrafted free agents are part of the study or excluded by design.
Make the modeling plan more informative than one regression line.
Right now the evaluation plan is very minimal. Expand it so the analysis feels like a real capstone paper:

Show summary tables by draft round.
Compare a baseline pick-only model to richer models with pre-draft controls.
Report both fit statistics and coefficient interpretations.
Check residual plots and look for influential outliers.
Use visuals that make the football story easy to understand.

A strong presentation figure would be a scatterplot of receiving yards versus draft pick with a flexible fitted curve and labels for notable late-round successes and early-round disappointments.
Consider whether there are important subgroup patterns.
One way to deepen the analysis is to test whether the relationship differs across contexts:

Before and after the 2011 rookie wage scale era, if you want a sports economics angle.
Early rounds versus later rounds, since front offices treat these groups very differently.
Power-conference versus non-power-conference college players, if college context is available.

You do not need to do all of these. Even one well-chosen heterogeneity analysis would make the capstone paper more interesting.
Include a few strong descriptive visuals in the capstone paper.
A small number of clean figures will make the project far more compelling:

A scatterplot of draft pick number versus first five seasons receiving yards, with a flexible fitted curve.
A box plot of receiving yards by draft round.
A bar chart or dot plot of the biggest “steals” and “busts” relative to the model’s prediction.
A coefficient plot if you estimate a multiple regression model.





References

Draw on key background readings and data sources.
For your capstone paper and presentation, you may find it helpful to consult, and where appropriate cite, the following sources:

Massey, C., & Thaler, R. H. (2013). “The Loser’s Curse: Decision Making and Market Efficiency in the National Football League Draft.” Management Science, 59(7), 1479-1495.
Teramoto, M., Cross, C. L., & Willick, S. E. (2016). “Predictive Value of National Football League Scouting Combine on Future Performance of Running Backs and Wide Receivers.” Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research, 30(5), 1379-1390.
Treme, J., & Allen, S. (2009). “Widely Received: Payoffs to Player Attributes in the NFL.” Economics Bluetin, 29(3), 1631-1643.
Hadley, B., Kim, J.W., Magnusen, M., & Kim, K.T. (2025). “Redefining the draft pick valuation in the National Football League.” Front Sports Act Living.
Kraeutler, M. J., Carver, T.J., Belk, J. W., & McCarty, E.C. (2018). “What is the Value of a National Football League Draft Pick? An Analysis Based on Changes Made in the Collective Bargaining Agreement.” Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research, 32(6), 1656-1661.
Pitts, J. & Evans, B. (2017). “Drafting for Success: How Good Are NFL Teams at Identifying Future Productivity at Offensive-Skill Positions in the Draft?.” The American Economist, 64(1), 102-122.
Pro Football Reference. NFL and AFL Draft History.
College Football at Sports-Reference.com. Data Coverage.
nflreadr documentation. nflreadr.





Questions to Think About as You Refine Your Capstone Paper
You do not need to answer all of these now, but they may help you sharpen the project.
1. What is the project really trying to measure?

Is the main question whether NFL teams are good at identifying future wide receiver talent?
Or is the question whether draft status itself shapes later production by giving some players more playing time, patience, and development opportunities?
Those are related, but not identical, questions. Your paper should make clear which one comes first.

2. What is the fairest outcome window?

Is a full-career total the right measure, or does it unfairly penalize more recent draft classes?
Would the first 3, 5, or 7 seasons be a more comparable window across players?

3. How will you handle players with very low opportunity?

If a receiver is drafted and never becomes part of a regular offense, is that a true zero that belongs in the sample?
Or does it create a different kind of outcome than the players who had real chances to produce?
Your answer affects both the modeling choice and the interpretation.

4. How much does the answer depend on the level of detail in the data?

If draft position alone predicts only a small share of the variation, does that mean the draft is inefficient?
Or does it simply mean that later NFL success depends on many post-draft factors such as scheme fit, quarterback quality, injuries, and coaching?

5. Could the paper tell a stronger story with one extension?

Would it be more interesting to compare wide receivers to another position group?
Would it be more interesting to study only first-contract production?
Would it be more interesting to compare raw production to efficiency measures?


Best,
Byeong-Hak Choe

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
Dear Theodore,
Thank you for your Research Kick-off Report. You have identified a timely and policy-relevant topic. Data centers are expanding quickly, and combining directory data with EPA EJSCREEN, ACS, ECHO, and New York State air permit records is a well-structured approach. The project also has a clear stakeholder audience: planners, community groups, and economic development offices all care about whether data center siting is associated with local environmental or neighborhood impacts.
Overall, this project has strong potential to become an original capstone project. The main priorities going forward are to narrow the scope, make the core empirical question more precise, and separate what your data can support as inference from what it can support as prediction.


Next Steps
You are working toward both the presentation and the capstone paper. Focus on a well-scoped empirical question, clear causal reasoning, and a primary method that is either inferential or predictive.

Decide whether the project is primarily about inference or prediction.
Right now, your research question is mostly inferential: are block groups near data centers systematically different from comparable block groups farther away? That is a reasonable capstone direction. If you keep that framing, the regression analysis should be the center of the paper, and the ML model should be secondary. If you prefer a prediction framing instead, you need one clearly defined prediction target, such as PM2.5 or median rent, and you need to evaluate out-of-sample performance by geography. Trying to do both equally may spread the project too thin.
Narrow and prioritize your outcome variables.
The proposal currently treats environmental, housing, and socioeconomic outcomes as parallel outcomes. That is too broad for one capstone project paper. For the paper, choose one primary family of outcomes and make the others secondary or descriptive.

Environmental indicators such as PM2.5, diesel particulate matter, and possibly traffic proximity are the most direct outcomes for your current design.
Housing measures such as median rent and home value are plausible, but they operate through different mechanisms and need a separate interpretation.
Poverty and unemployment are more likely to describe where data centers locate than to be caused by data centers in a cross-sectional design. A good strategy would be to make PM2.5 or diesel PM your primary outcome and treat housing outcomes as a secondary extension.

Define “quality of life” more precisely.
“Quality of life” is a broad phrase, but your actual variables are specific neighborhood indicators. In the capstone paper, define exactly what you mean. For example, you might write that you are studying environmental and neighborhood indicators associated with proximity to data centers, rather than claiming to measure overall quality of life directly. That makes the paper more precise and easier to defend.
Address the main confounding problem directly.
The biggest challenge in this project is that data centers do not locate randomly. They often cluster near existing industrial land, substations, fiber routes, dense commercial zones, and transportation infrastructure. Those same places may already have different air quality, rent levels, and demographic composition.
For the capstone paper:

Control for nearby industrial activity using ECHO facility counts, air permit counts, or similar measures.
Include metro area fixed effects or county fixed effects so you are comparing nearby and farther block groups within the same broader market rather than comparing Manhattan to rural upstate areas.
Use population density, median income, housing stock characteristics, and other ACS variables as controls.
Consider restricting the sample to metros or counties where data centers are actually present, so the comparison group is more plausible.
Be explicit that even with controls, the results are best interpreted as associations with cautious causal reasoning, not definitive proof of causal effects, unless you can add timing.

Try to add timing if you can.
Right now the design is cross-sectional. If you can identify approximate opening years for even a subset of facilities, your paper becomes much stronger. That would let you compare nearby areas before and after openings using multiple ACS 5-year releases or annual EJSCREEN vintages. Even a limited before-after analysis for a smaller set of verified facilities would be stronger than a pure cross-section.
If opening dates are not feasible, keep the design cross-sectional and be disciplined about the claims you make. Also note that ACS 5-year releases overlap, so small year-to-year changes should be interpreted cautiously.
Clarify what counts as a data center.
A major measurement issue is that “data center” can mean very different things. A small colocation facility in a dense urban building is not the same exposure as a large standalone campus with major backup generation. If possible, distinguish facilities by type, size, or a proxy such as air permit intensity, generator permits, or facility operator category. If that is not possible, at least discuss this limitation clearly in the paper.
Be precise about the treatment variable and spatial matching rule.
Distance to the nearest data center is a good starting point, but you should think carefully about how to model it and how to construct it.

A simple continuous distance variable may be too restrictive.
Distance bands such as within 1 mile, 1-3 miles, 3-5 miles, and more than 5 miles may be easier to interpret.
You could also use data center count within a buffer as an alternative exposure measure.
Be clear about whether distance is measured from the block group centroid or from the block group boundary.
For environmental pathways, straight-line distance is reasonable. For traffic or access-related pathways, road-network distance may be more appropriate.
Explain how you handle block groups that actually contain a data center.

I would recommend testing both nearest-distance and buffer-count measures and then choosing one as your primary specification.
Write out the regression model explicitly.
For the capstone project paper, state the inferential model clearly. A natural baseline specification is:
\[Y_{b} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \text{NearDataCenter}_{b} + \mathbf{X}_{b}'\boldsymbol{\gamma} + \mu_m + \varepsilon_b\]
where \(b\) indexes census block groups, \(\text{NearDataCenter}_{b}\) is your distance- or buffer-based exposure measure, \(\mathbf{X}_{b}\) includes controls such as income, density, housing variables, and nearby industrial activity, and \(\mu_m\) is a metro-area fixed effect.
If you keep a continuous distance measure, you may want to use \(\log(\text{distance}+1)\) or distance bands so the model does not impose a completely linear effect.
Use EJSCREEN variables carefully.
EJSCREEN is very useful, but some of its outputs are screening indicators or percentiles, not direct measured exposures. For your main regressions, prefer the raw environmental indicators rather than percentile rankings when possible. Percentiles are harder to interpret and can mix the underlying environmental burden with broader comparison-based scaling. Also, be careful not to treat EJSCREEN as hyper-local ground-monitor data. It is a screening tool.
Be realistic about geographic resolution.
Block groups give you more spatial detail, but ACS block-group estimates can be noisy, especially for housing and income measures outside large metros. If the block-group data become unstable or difficult to merge cleanly, census tracts may be a better primary unit. It is better to have a cleaner, more defensible tract-level analysis than an overcomplicated block-group analysis that becomes fragile.
Account for spatial dependence in your uncertainty estimates.
Nearby block groups are not independent. They share pollution, land use, and housing market conditions. At minimum:

Cluster standard errors at the county or metro level.
Explain how you avoid spatial leakage in any predictive exercise.
If you keep an ML component, hold out an entire metro area rather than random block groups.

Reconsider the alternative energy sub-question.
Your third sub-question on co-located renewables or small modular reactors is interesting, but it is not well integrated with the current dataset. As written, it reads more like a policy discussion than an empirical research question. I would recommend one of two paths:

Move it to the policy implications section at the end of the paper, or
Reframe it as a testable empirical question by adding county- or region-level electricity data from an official energy source. Unless you add that extra data, it should not be one of the central empirical questions.

Clean up the source list and verify official links.
Several of the links in the kickoff report are archived snapshot pages or marketplace directories. For the capstone project paper, keep the commercial directories as source leads, but cite the most stable official documentation for the government datasets. In particular, use:

ACS API documentation from the U.S. Census Bureau
ECHO data downloads from EPA Web Archive
The New York State Open Data air permits dataset
Current EJSCREEN technical documentation

For the commercial directory sources, the stable New York pages are DataCenterMap - New York and Inflect - New York, United States. Use these as discovery tools, not as final proof that a facility exists.
A simpler prediction component is acceptable if you want one.
If you keep an ML component, make it narrow and clearly secondary. For example, you could predict PM2.5 using block-group controls plus data center exposure measures, and evaluate whether adding the data center variables improves out-of-sample RMSE when you hold out an entire metro area. That would give you a clean prediction exercise that complements, rather than replaces, the regression-based inference.
Include a few strong descriptive visuals.
Before the regression results, the paper and presentation would benefit from:

a map of New York State showing verified data center locations
a table comparing block groups by distance group
a binscatter (e.g., geom_hex()) or grouped mean plot of PM2.5 against distance to the nearest data center within metro areas These will make the project easier to understand and help the audience see why the inferential model matters.





Questions to Think About as You Refine Your Capstone Project Paper
You do not need to answer all of these, but they may help you sharpen the analysis and policy narrative.
1. What is the strongest outcome for your design?

Which outcome is closest to a plausible mechanism from data center operations: PM2.5, diesel PM, noise, traffic exposure, rent, or home value?
Which of these can you measure most credibly with the data you actually have?

2. What makes two communities “comparable”?

If you compare block groups near and far from data centers, how will you make sure you are not just comparing industrial urban cores to suburban or rural areas?
Would a within-metro comparison with strong controls be more credible than a statewide pooled comparison?

3. What counts as exposure?

Is the right exposure measure distance to the nearest facility, number of facilities within a buffer, or a weighted exposure measure based on facility size?
Should all data centers be treated equally, or do large generator-heavy facilities deserve more weight than small colocation sites?

4. What can you say about causality?

If you find worse environmental indicators near data centers, how will you separate the effect of the data center itself from the fact that these facilities often locate in already industrial places?
Is there any way to recover opening years or permit dates for a subset of facilities to strengthen the causal story?

5. How should the ML piece fit the paper?

If you use random forest or LASSO, what exactly is the prediction target?
What would the prediction model add beyond the regression analysis?
Would it be better to keep the paper centered on inference and use the ML component only as a short robustness or extension section?

6. What are the policy implications?

If you find no clear association between proximity and your primary environmental outcome, does that suggest current permitting is adequate, or only that your data are too coarse to detect very local harms?
If you do find evidence that communities near data centers face worse environmental conditions, what specific planning tools would follow from that result: tighter permitting, buffer guidance, generator restrictions, community benefit agreements, or better monitoring?




Background Readings and Source Documentation
For your capstone project paper and presentation, the following sources would be useful background:

IEA. (2025). Energy and AI. (Useful background on the rapid growth of electricity demand tied to AI and data centers.)
Masanet, E., Shehabi, A., Lei, N., Smith, S., & Koomey, J. (2020). “Recalibrating Global Data Center Energy-Use Estimates.” Science, 367(6481), 984-986.
Currie, J., Davis, L., Greenstone, M., & Walker, R. (2015). “Environmental Health Risks and Housing Values: Evidence from 1,600 Toxic Plant Openings and Closings.” American Economic Review, 105(2), 678-709.
O’Donnell, J. & Crownhart, C. (2025). “We did the math on AI’s energy footprint. Here’s the story you haven’t heard.” MIT Technology Review
Xiao, T., Nerini, F.F., Matthews, H.D. et al. (2025). “Environmental impact and net-zero pathways for sustainable artificial intelligence servers in the USA” Nature Sustainability, 8, 1541–1553.
U.S. EPA. EJScreen Technical Documentation.
U.S. Census Bureau. American Community Survey Data via API.
U.S. EPA. ECHO Data Downloads.
New York State Open Data. Issued State Facility Air Permits.
News-style articles to illustrate current debates and policy concerns:

“A.I. Is on the Rise, and So Is the Environmental Impact of the Data Centers That Drive It” (Smithsonian Magazine)
“Responding to the climate impact of generative AI” (MIT News)

“AI has an environmental problem. Here’s what the world can do about that.” (UNEP) - “AI, data centers, and water” (Brookings Institution)


Best,
Byeong-Hak Choe
